entries.title: Englewood dad fatally shot in his home was family's 'protector,' fiancé says
entries.summary: <p>Jennifer Lacey felt like 2026 was going to be her year. </p><p>She had just gotten promoted at work. Her kids were doing well. And she had just moved into a new home with her fiancé, Antwan Washington.</p><p>Then her 11-year-old son woke up to find bullet holes in the living room wall and his dad motionless on the couch. At first, Lacey thought he was asleep.</p><p>"From the position he was sitting, it looked like he was asleep," Lacey said. "So I tapped him, like, 'Babe, get up. You have to get to work.' ... He just falls to the right, and that's when I see that he was shot in the head. ... Just the worst day of my life, ever."</p><p>Washington, 45, had been shot in the head and was pronounced dead on the scene, according to Chicago police.</p><p>He was in his home watching TV in the 5700 block of South May Street around 5 a.m. Saturday when two people got out of a gray 4-door sedan and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/28/man-fatally-shot-inside-englewood-home" >fired handguns into the apartment</a>, according to a police report. The car fled the scene. Police found 16 shell casings in the intersection.</p><p>"He wasn't doing anything but doing his normal morning routine: getting up in the morning, you know, washing up, brushing his teeth, you know, getting dressed and making his coffee and smoking his cigarette," Lacey, 33, said through tears.</p><p>The police report states that someone else in the building was the intended target of the shooting.</p><p>"There's really no motivation for it," Lacey said. "We're just a working family just wanting to raise our kids." </p><p>Now Lacey is grappling with the aftermath of his death. She must now raise their four sons without their father. Her future plans are shattered: a housewarming party at their new home, hosting family for barbecues and watching their kids grow up. Washington also leaves behind a 21-year-old son from a previous relationship, she said.</p><p>"I will continue to let my kids know how much he did love them, how much of a leader he was, and how much positivity he instilled in them as children," she said.</p><p>Lacey described Washington as a "protector" who worked hard and loved his family. He could often be found playing video games with his sons, barbecuing in the summer and listening to Earth, Wind & Fire or 2Pac. He was a manager at an Englewood Chipotle and had recently moved to manage a team at a Bridgeport location, Lacey said.</p><p>No one is in custody, police said.</p><p>A <a class="Link" href="https://www.gofundme.com/f/support-for-antwans-family-after-tragic-loss" target="_blank" >GoFundMe</a> set up by his fiancée had raised $2,500 of a $10,000 goal by Monday night. It said the "simple moments meant everything to him and his family."</p><p><i>Contributing: Violet Miller</i></p>
entries.author: Mary Norkol

entries.title: Miguel Vargas’ grand slam helps White Sox shake off season-opening ‘jitters’ for first win
entries.summary: <p>MIAMI — Maybe they just needed a few extra days to warm up.</p><p>After only showing signs of life late in a disastrous opening weekend, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="_blank" >White Sox</a> hitters found their stride Monday against the Marlins, clubbing their way to a 9-4 victory for their first triumph of the season.</p><p>A monster six-RBI night from Miguel Vargas in humid Miami made the Sox’ season-opening misery in blustery Milwaukee feel hundreds of miles away, even if their pitching woes kept things more interesting than fans would like.</p><p>Everson Pereira warmed up the Sox in the third inning by lining a double down the left-field line. He later scored on a single by Vargas.</p><p>Munetaka Murakami didn’t extend his historic three-game home-run streak to start his MLB career, but he did notch his first hit to stay in the park on a dribbler down the third-base line that set the stage for a three-run homer by Austin Hays, his first blast since <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/21/white-sox-outfielder-austin-hays-brings-steadying-force-to-lineup" target="_blank" ><u>signing with the Sox</u></a>.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Stay fair. IT WILL ❗️ <a href="https://t.co/cMZwl8mN2D">pic.twitter.com/cMZwl8mN2D</a></p>— Chicago White Sox (@whitesox) <a href="https://twitter.com/whitesox/status/2038760220638224720?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 30, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>In the fourth, Marlins starter Chris Paddack hit Luisangel Acuna with a pitch to load the bases after singles by Tristan Peters and Pereira. Then Vargas, a native of Cuba who lives in Miami, launched one 402 feet into the left-field seats for the Sox’ <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/29/white-sox-colson-montgomery-grand-slam-murakami-homer" target="_blank" ><u>second grand slam in as many days</u></a>. He had dozens of supporters in attendance at loanDepot Park.</p><p>‘‘Especially to have my family here and a couple of friends that never watched me play in the big leagues, it’s super-fun, and I’m very proud to accomplish what we accomplished today,’’ Vargas said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">MIGUEL VARGAS GRAND SLAM<br><br>LET’S GOOOOOO <a href="https://t.co/gmGOmxMhG5">pic.twitter.com/gmGOmxMhG5</a></p>— Mike (@ChiSoxFanMike) <a href="https://twitter.com/ChiSoxFanMike/status/2038764128932676030?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 30, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>But no lead feels particularly comfortable for Sox pitchers, and right-hander Davis Martin served up a two-run homer to Liam Hicks in the fourth. The Marlins struck again in the fifth with an RBI single by Jakob Marsee.</p><p>All told, Martin allowed three runs and five hits with six strikeouts and two walks in five innings.</p><p></p><p>The Sox added a run in the sixth when Acuna walked, stole second and third and then slid home on a shallow sacrifice fly by Vargas, his sixth RBI of the evening.</p><p></p><p>Offseason addition Sean Newcomb was strong in relief until a triple by Marsee and a single by Otto Lopez narrowed the Sox’ lead to 9-4 in the eighth. He left a bases-loaded jam for Jordan Hicks to shut down.</p><p></p><p>After thwacking a grand slam in a losing cause when the Sox’ bullpen melted down Sunday against the Brewers, Colson Montgomery said he and other Sox hitters finally were shaking off <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/28/colson-montgomery-white-sox-year-2" target="_blank" ><u>some early-season nerves</u></a>. After being outscored 20-3 in their first two games, the Sox have stacked up 16 runs in two days.</p><p></p><p>‘‘We all progressively just kept getting better,’’ said Montgomery, who needed 74 career games to sock his first two grand slams, the fastest in Sox history. ‘‘We are seeing the ball a lot better, having better-quality at-bats. I think it was the jitters and we were all really excited.’’</p><p></p><p>Pereira acknowledged as much before his second consecutive multihit game after flailing through three strikeouts on Opening Day.</p><p></p><p>‘‘I’m one of those players that is really excited, and I want to crush every single pitch,’’ Pereira said. ‘‘Just try to slow the game down and breathe.’’</p><p></p><p>Now the Sox are hoping the offensive results multiply.</p><p></p><p>‘‘It’s just part of the game,’’ manager Will Venable said. ‘‘You spend all this time in spring training and going about your business, and then the lights come on and it means something and it changes a little bit. You’ve just got to find your bearings again.’’</p><p></p><p>Vargas found his bearings while borrowing Murakami’s first-baseman’s mitt.</p><p></p><p>‘‘He got three homers in three games, and now I use it and I got one,’’ Vargas said. ‘‘So I might be keeping it with me.’’</p><div class="PageListL" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" entries.title: White Sox players calling ‘em like they feel ‘em under MLB’s new ABS challenge system
entries.summary: <p>MIAMI — The first several games of the automated ball/strike era have been a mixed bag for the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="_blank" ><u>White Sox</u></a>.</p><p>Catcher <b>Edgar Quero</b> hasn’t been shy about questioning umpires under the new ABS challenge system implemented <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/sports-media/2026/02/12/how-abs-challenge-system-will-affect-mlb-tv-broadcasts-chicago-cubs-white-sox-spring-training-schedules" target="_blank" ><u>this season</u></a>, but Sox hitters still are feeling it out.</p><p>‘‘We’ve seen them less willing to pull the trigger on some challenges,’’ manager <b>Will Venable</b> said. ‘‘And I don’t get instant feedback on all these pitches on where they’re at, but it seems like we’re seeing less challenges from hitters.’’</p><p>Through four games, <b>Lenyn Sosa</b>, <b>Tristan Peters</b> and <b>Munetaka Murakami</b> are the only Sox batters to challenge calls. Sosa and Peters were wrong, but Murakami wiped out a miscalled strike Monday.</p><p>Quero has challenged nine calls behind the plate, getting four bad ones overturned. Sox pitchers have yet to initiate a review.</p><p>So should the Sox be quicker to tap their hat or helmet?</p><p>‘‘If they’re right, yeah,’’ Venable said with a laugh. ‘‘We want to make sure we’re challenging the right pitches. . . . It’s still new for a lot of these guys.’’</p><p>Venable didn’t tip the Sox’ ABS playbook, but he said ‘‘a mid-at-bat challenge is different than a potential strikeout or walk at-bat-ending challenge. There’s different things, different parts of the zone that we’ve talked about, different guidelines.’’</p><p>Quero said he’ll keep going with his gut.</p><p>‘‘Yeah, [based on] good feelings and . . . seeing the ball, where it’s coming from, and knowing the strike zone a little bit better,’’ he said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Anthony Kay throws a pitch against the Milwaukee Brewers at American Family Field on March 29.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>John Fisher/Getty Images</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><b>Long road back</b></h3><p>Left-hander <b>Anthony Kay’s</b> start Sunday in Milwaukee was his first big-league action in 2½ years. He joined the Sox on <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/02/19/white-sox-anthony-kay-looks-to-carry-over-success-from-japan" target="_blank" ><u>a two-year, $12 million contract</u></a> during the offseason after rejuvenating his career with a stint in Japan.</p><p>‘‘I’ve just grown so much mentally those couple of years in Japan,’’ Kay, 31, said. ‘‘That’s been a huge difference for me in my career.’’</p><p>It wasn’t pretty, but Kay turned in the best start of the Sox’ abysmal opening series, giving up two runs and three hits, striking out five and walking four in 4⅔ innings.</p><p>‘‘Definitely got a little emotional walking out to the bullpen before the game,’’ Kay said. ‘‘Definitely was a little amped up and had to hone that in a little bit. I think I did a good job of settling in after that.’’</p><p>It wasn’t exactly the season-opening sensation the Sox have gotten from a certain other Japanese veteran on their roster, but they are relying on Kay to eat up some serious innings this season while waiting for a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/02/12/noah-schultz-hagen-smith-white-sox-spring-training-triple-a" target="_blank" ><u>a wave of talented arms</u></a> to emerge from Triple-A Charlotte.</p><p>Kay got a preview of new teammate <b>Munetaka Murakami’s</b> prodigious power while facing him overseas.</p><p>‘‘I’m not surprised by it,’’ Kay said of Murakami’s <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/29/white-sox-colson-montgomery-grand-slam-murakami-homer" target="_blank" ><u>three home runs</u></a> in his first three MLB games. ‘‘I know some people are, but I’ve seen it.’’</p><h3>Numbers game</h3><p>Before Opening Day, Quero switched his jersey number from the 7 he debuted with last season to 26, which he said holds personal significance as a combination of the birthdates of his mother, his sister and his own.</p><p>It’s also the number catcher <b>Korey Lee</b> wore for the Sox until being designated for assignment at the end of spring training, ending up as <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/22/white-sox-sign-catcher-reese-mcguire-leaving-korey-lee-as-odd-man-out" target="_blank" ><u>the odd man out</u></a> when general manager <b>Chris Getz</b> signed superior pitch-framer <b>Reese McGuire</b>.</p><p>Lee cleared waivers and now is wearing No. 26 at Charlotte.</p><div class="PageListL" entries.author: Mitchell Armentrout

entries.title: Michael Busch has grown into indispensable member of Cubs' lineup
entries.summary: <p>In Michael Busch’s first at-bat Monday night against the Angels at Wrigley Field, he walked on four pitches that didn’t come close to the strike zone.</p><p>After a breakout 2025 season that saw Busch finish third among first basemen in homers (34), seventh in RBI (90) and second in wRC+ (140), he has earned that respect from pitchers because they know he capitalizes on mistakes. So far this season, Busch has maintained his productive ways at the plate.</p><p>Busch isn’t the showman Pete Crow-Armstrong is, nor is he the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs" >Cubs</a>’ clubhouse leader like Dansby Swanson or the All-Star like Alex Bregman. He’s an unassuming player who lets his game speak for itself — and it’s loud.</p><p>“He was the best first baseman in baseball last year,” Crow-Armstrong told the Sun-Times before the Cubs’ 7-2 victory. “I know you still got [Dodgers first baseman Freddie Freeman] and [Orioles slugger Pete Alonso]. But last year, I don’t think there was a better first baseman in baseball.”</p><p>Busch fits in with the measured and workmanlike group that team president of baseball operations Jed Hoyer has assembled. The Cubs traded left-handed pitching prospect Jackson Ferris and outfield prospect Zyhir Hope to the Dodgers for reliever Yence Almonte and Busch before the 2024 season.</p><p>At the time of the trade, Busch was the Dodgers’ No. 2 prospect, according to MLB.com, but was blocked from regular playing time because of their star-studded cast of future Hall of Famers at first and second base, and the designated hitter spot.</p><p>With regular playing time, Busch has flourished in Chicago. And now, after manager Craig Counsell said he’s going to use him more against left-handers, he’ll have even more responsibility, and Busch should fare well. He already sets the tone for the team as the leadoff hitter, even though he doesn’t fit the prototypical model of one. Crow-Armstrong said jokingly that Busch is “lacking a little bit of speed.”</p><p>“I love him in the leadoff spot,” Crow-Armstrong said. “His ability to get on base, and especially when he’s hot, makes things feel like an event.”</p><p>He’s a selective hitter with plus power and rarely chases. Crow-Armstrong lauded Busch for his work in the batting cages, saying that the first baseman works at a “very controlled level,” and that when the tempo speeds up in the game, he’s prepared for it.</p><p>“[Busch] knows what he can handle, and he does a really good job of swinging at the pitches that are right for him,” left fielder Ian Happ told the Sun-Times. “It’s not as easy as it sounds to do that. On Opening Day [Busch reached base four times and was 3-for-4 with two doubles] his ability to control the zone and get the pitches that he wanted to hit was impressive.”</p><p>Busch has room for improvement on defense. He graded out in the 36th percentile in outs above average.</p><p>“Think just being a little more efficient in all areas,” Busch said. “Especially [with] my footwork over at first, which is one thing that I want to take pride in, making sure I’m really comfortable with how I’m getting to the base, working around the base.”</p><p>But if Busch’s 2025 performance is his floor, the Cubs are in a good spot. He doesn’t garner the recognition of his teammates, but they understand Busch’s importance to the lineup.</p><bsp-listcarousel class="PageListE" data-module data-column-count=3 entries.author: Kyle Williams

entries.title: Onetime Spurs guard Tre Jones talks 'Iceman' nickname and Caleb Williams
entries.summary: <p>SAN ANTONIO – Tre Jones spent five-plus seasons wearing Spurs black and silver, learning the history of the organization.</p><p>The Bulls guard has also spent the last few years in Chicago, and doing so as a football fan of the Bears and quarterback Caleb Williams.</p><p>Those two worlds collided as Jones found himself stuck going between historian and politician.</p><p>There’s been a lot made of the “Iceman” nickname the last week, especially since in San Antonio there was only one “Iceman:” NBA legend George Gervin.</p><p>Williams tried to hijack the nickname and claim the legal copyright, which didn’t sit well in many circles, including with Gervin.</p><p>Jones has two horses in the race so was guarded on how he approached the entire saga.</p><p>“Yeah, the ‘Iceman,’ “ Jones said when Gervin’s name was mentioned. “George Gervin was a pioneer for basketball. Obviously, not only for (the Spurs) franchise, but the entire NBA as well. He pushed the envelope in so many ways.</p><p>“I think that as long as ‘The Iceman’ nickname isn’t disrespected and it’s kept as a sign of respect to George Gervin … you know it’s a new eras, a new generation, names can keep flying around like that.”</p><p>Plus, Jones and his Bulls teammates had a bigger nickname to worry about on Monday: “Wemby.”</p><p>Looking to avoid a winless four-game road trip, the Bulls watched current Spurs legend Victor Wembanyama put on a clinic on both ends of the floor, scoring 41 points, grabbing 16 rebounds and adding three blocks to his box score, as the home team ran away with it, 129-114.</p><p>Not that it hurt the big picture for Billy Donovan’s crew, as they dropped a half-game behind idle Milwaukee in the standings, but kept the No. 9 spot in the draft lottery odds.</p><p>And frankly after the first quarter, they were never really a threat to challenge the home team. The Bulls played San Antonio even in the first quarter 29-28, but Wembanyama and second-year guard Stephon Castle took over, combining for 23 points in the second quarter.</p><p>Not a good way to end a day that started with the news that Jaden Ivey was let go for behavior detrimental to the team.</p><p>“He’s a very talented player,” guard Josh Giddey said of the Ivey news. “Obviously the whole thing is kind of unfortunate in a way. I hope he gets the help he needs, whatever he’s going through or not going through. I do really hope he gets help. It’s not going to be with the Bulls anymore, but wherever it is I hope he gets it.”</p><p>Meanwhile, as far as the “Iceman” debate, Williams has been courtside for several Bulls games since being drafted by the Bears two years ago, and that hasn’t gone unnoticed by Bulls players. That’s why as much as Jones respects the tradition and history of San Antonio, he admittedly offered up support in what the quarterback was attempting to do.</p><p>“I like Caleb a lot,” Jones said. “He had a great year this year and the ‘Iceman’ nickname was really cool. I support that as well, but as long as there’s the respect that George Gervin deserves with it.”</p><p></p><p></p><h3>More on Jones</h3><p></p><p>Gervin wasn’t the only topic Jones had to address before the game.</p><p></p><p>Like his older brother, <b>Tyus</b>, Jones played at Duke, still bleeds blue and was horrified to watch his alma mater become the first No. 1 seed in the NCAA Tournament to lose after leading by at least 15 points at the half.</p><p></p><p>‘‘That was a tough one for sure,’’ Jones said of the game Sunday. ‘‘Last year was tough, missing free throws and the turnover at the end of the game. But this year, up 19 . . . I was back at the hotel with my family, and it was definitely tough.</p><p></p><p>‘‘I always back them. I think this is setting them up for something down the line, for sure. [Coach <b>Jon</b>] <b>Scheyer </b>has been to three straight Elite Eights now. Final Four last year. Moments away from a Final Four this year. So I think whatever is happening is just preparing them for the future, for sure.’’</p><p></p><h3>Fixer-upper</h3><p></p><p>Bulls coach <b>Billy Donovan </b>has seen a lot of positives from forward <b>Leonard Miller</b>.</p><p></p><p>The high-energy Miller joined the Bulls from the Timberwolves in the <b>Ayo Dosunmu</b> trade and has worked his way into the rotation and even some starts.</p><p></p><p>Donovan, however, wants to see him take the next step in his game.</p><p></p><p>‘‘It’s just, like, the detail of who’s he guarding? What coverage is he in? Who is in the action with him? When is he switching?’’ Donovan said. ‘‘Those are things that are happening fast where he can get caught there several times. We’ve got to help him get better.’’</p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-hide-descriptions="true" data-show-loadmore="true" ><a class="AnchorLink" id="list-8f0005" name="list-8f0005"></a><div class="PageList-header" > <div class="PageList-header-title-wrap" > <div class="PageList-header-title"> <a class="" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bulls" target="{borderColor=#dfdfdf, pageNumber=1, inverseColors=false, footerBgColor=#000000, bodyFont=font-3, showListNumbers=false, imagePositionHorizontal=null, rteBodyFont=font-3, headerBottomBorderColor=#111010, subTitle=null, stickyHeader=true, listTitleColor=#000000, tableCellBorderColor=#ccd1d5, relatedListFont=font-1, items=[{type=Image, publishDate=March 30, 2026 06:29 PM, title=Bulls waive guard Jaden Ivey after anti-Pride remarks, category=[{items=[Bulls]}], _styledTemplate=null, authors=[{bio=[{items=[Joe Cowley covers the Chicago Bulls for the Chicago Sun-Times.]}], ampLink=null, _styledTemplate=null, _template=/author/byline/AuthorByline.hbs, media=[{narrowImage={src=https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/bd/9b/8fe15ac7524e52710ea7cf4aed7f/9-15-lachat-cowley-4-0.jpg, width=512, height=512}, alt=9-15-lachat-cowley-4-0.jpg, image={src=https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/bd/9b/8fe15ac7524e52710ea7cf4aed7f/9-15-lachat-cowley-4-0.jpg, width=512, height=512}, ampLink=null, _styledTemplate=null, _template=/image/Image.hbs}], name=[{items=[Joe Cowley]}], jobTitle=[{items=[Chicago Bulls reporter]}], authorPageUrl=https://chicago.suntimes.com/authors/joe-cowley}], imageAlignment=null, isFeatured=false, categoryUrl=https://chicago.suntimes.com/bulls, description=[{items=[Since being acquired from the Pistons in the Kevin Huerter deal, Ivey has gone on a series of rants about religion. entries.title: Horoscope for Tuesday, March 31, 2026
entries.summary: <h2>Moon Alert</h2> <p>Avoid shopping (except food and gas) or major decisions from 7 p.m. to 10:15 p.m. After that, the moon moves from Virgo into Libra.</p><h2>Aries (March 21-April 19)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Give yourself extra time today so that you have room to deal with surprises related to work, your health or your pet. Something unexpected with high-tech equipment, co-workers or schedules might present itself. Stay off your heels. Keep your eyes open.</p><h2>Taurus (April 20-May 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> A surprise invitation might come your way today. If so, be ready to act quickly because you want some adventure. Parents should be vigilant about their kids because something unexpected could impact them or their schedules. Guard against sports accidents.</p><h2>Gemini (May 21-June 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Your home routine might change today. Stock the fridge because someone unexpected could drop by. Small appliances might break down or a minor breakage could occur. Or something delightful and unexpected might please you. (Let's hope.)</p><h2>Cancer (June 21-July 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Pay attention to everything you say and do today because your daily routine might change. (This could be fun.) You might see new places and meet new faces. You might have a brilliant idea because you can think out of the box. Give yourself extra time today to meet the unexpected.</p><h2>Leo (July 23-Aug. 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Today you might find money; you might lose money. Be smart and protect what you have against loss, theft or damage. On the upside, you might have a clever moneymaking idea. You might also have a chance to buy something that delights you. (Could be anything.)</p><h2>Virgo (Aug. 23-Sept. 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Today the moon is in your sign dancing with unpredictable Uranus, which is why you probably want emotional excitement. Seek out people who are different from you. Make some needed changes in your immediate environment. You might become a leader and advocate for new policies.</p><h2>Libra (Sept. 23-Oct. 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> This is a restless day for you. You don't have to figure out why — just relax. It's a temporary, hidden stimulation, so just go along with it. Fortunately, you have the energy to work hard and get better organized today. Romantic relationships will also be a fun distraction.</p><h2>Scorpio (Oct. 23-Nov. 21)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> You might meet someone unusual today. Or perhaps someone you already know will say or do something that catches you off guard. It could be a clever, engaging surprise, or you might be amazed at what they do. A group of people advocating for change will attract you.</p><h2>Sagittarius (Nov. 22-Dec. 21)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> By nature, you are an independent soul. Today, in particular, you will fight for your independence and freedom, which is why an interaction with a parent, boss or someone in authority might be surprising. You want the freedom to do your own thing — that's all.</p><h2>Capricorn (Dec. 22-Jan. 19)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Travel plans might change today, or perhaps you might suddenly decide to travel somewhere. You might encounter someone who is different. They might also be exciting and provocative. Revolutionary ideas will appeal to you today.</p><h2>Aquarius (Jan. 20-Feb. 18)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Double-check banking details and anything to do with shared property, insurance issues, wills and debt because something unexpected could impact these areas. Perhaps a fraudulent charge on your credit card? You snooze, you lose.</p><h2>Pisces (Feb. 19-March 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> A spouse, partner or close friend might throw you a curveball today or do something you least expect. If you're lucky, it's a fun invitation that pleases you — perhaps a treat? Or you might learn information about them that throws a whole new light on something. Don't overreact. Today you have to go along to get along. (No biggie.)</p><h2>If today is your birthday</h2><p>Actor Ewan McGregor (1971) shares your birthday today. You are a steadfast realist who is resourceful. You are calm and in control. This year you will receive recognition for your past efforts. You might attain power and leadership. Some of you can expect the spotlight, acknowledgment, kudos and awards. This is a busy year for you!</p>
entries.author: Georgia Nicols

entries.title: West Side man gunned down in West Pullman
entries.summary: <p>Nearly 90 shell casings were recovered from the scene where a man was fatally shot Monday morning in West Pullman on the Far South Side. </p><p>Jajauan Thigpen, 26, was in the street in the 800 block of West 122nd Street about 11:55 a.m. when he was shot multiple times in the body, Chicago police and the Cook County medical examiner's office said. </p><p>Thigpen, of the city's Garfield Park neighborhood, was pronounced dead at the scene at 12:04 p.m., officials said. </p><p>About 86 shell casings were recovered from the scene, a police source said. </p><p>Area 2 detectives were investigating. </p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: 2 fatally shot during fight in East Garfield Park home; 1 in custody
entries.summary: <p>Charges were pending for a man who was arrested after a man and a woman were fatally shot during a fight Monday night in an East Garfield Park home. </p><p>Two men, 44 and 31, began fighting around 9:40 p.m. at the home in the 2800 block of West Washington Boulevard when the older man pulled out a knife and attacked the younger man, Chicago police said. </p><p>But the younger man, who has a valid firearm owner identification card, pulled a gun and fired multiple shots, hitting the knife-wielding man multiple times in the body, police said. A 38-year-old woman trying to intervene was shot in the head. </p><p>Both gunshot victims were taken to Mount Sinai Hospital where they were pronounced dead, officials said. </p><p>A firearm was recovered at the scene. </p><p>The FOID card holder was in custody and charges were pending. </p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Dear Abby: Woman leaves little to the imagination with comment to son
entries.summary: <p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> My wife and I are disgusted by something we (and our grandson) have never before encountered. Our ex-daughter-in-law of 10 years recently told our 16-year-old grandson she kicked her latest boyfriend out because he would not have sex with her. Our grandson was very upset (as were we). Isn't this considered some sort of abuse?</p><p>Our grandson and his brother haven't lived with their mother for the last five years because they were tired of her smoking, drinking and revolving door of boyfriends. They live happily with their father and have little to do with their mother. — <i>DISGUSTED IN COLORADO</i></p><p><b>DEAR DISGUSTED:</b> Your grandson is a teenager; he isn't 8. That his mother would say what she did to him is surprising, but it wasn't "abuse." Could she have been tipsy when she said it? Fortunately, your grandson no longer lives under her roof and has a father with better judgment. Count your blessings and let this pass.</p><p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> My boyfriend carries his keys and several metal accessories on a carabiner, which is always clipped to his belt loop. He lives in my home and is a frequent passenger in my car. I have noticed that his key clip and other items are damaging the paint on my car and door frames at the house as he exits and enters. I have asked him to please remove the clip or make another arrangement for his keys, and although he says he's "working on the problem," nothing changes. I'm ready to refuse entry to this key monster. Advice? — <i>DINGED IN OHIO</i></p><p><b>DEAR DINGED:</b> Only this. It seems that your irresponsible boyfriend lacks respect for other people's property or he would have dealt with this promptly. Estimate the cost of refinishing your car and door frames, and then offer him the opportunity to settle up what he owes you, or tell him it's time to move.</p><p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> One of our co-workers doesn't want to be a closer friend even though at work we say we love each other. We want to take the friendship to a deeper level, but she hesitates when we try to hang out outside of work. She has a mansion that many people in our office have seen, but she hasn't invited us over. She'll only go to the movies with us. We are worried it is because, in a movie theater, we can't talk to her. Are we getting mixed messages or coming on too strong? — <i>GIRLS AT WORK IN MICHIGAN</i></p><p><b>DEAR GIRLS:</b> You are coming on way too strong. There are work relationships and personal relationships. They are not always interchangeable. The woman may "love" working with you. But to assume that it's OK to pressure her into inviting you to her home or to be grilled about her personal life is wrong. You may mean well, but you girls need to accept the relationship as it is.</p><p><i>Dear Abby is written by Abigail Van Buren, also known as Jeanne Phillips, and was founded by her mother, Pauline Phillips. Contact Dear Abby at www.DearAbby.com or P.O. Box 69440, Los Angeles, CA 90069.</i></p><p>Abby shares more than 100 of her favorite recipes in two booklets: "Abby's Favorite Recipes" and "More Favorite Recipes by Dear Abby." Send your name and mailing address, plus check or money order for $16 (U.S. funds) to: Dear Abby, Cookbooklet Set, P.O. Box 446, Kings Mills, OH 45034-0446. (Shipping and handling are included in the price.)</p>
entries.author: Abigail Van Buren

entries.title: Popular vote plan to elect presidents is patriotic
entries.summary: <p>This year, America marks 250 years since the Declaration of Independence.</p><p>That should make us proud. It should also make us honest.</p><p>When my son was 11, we walked the Lexington battlefield in Massachusetts. As we crossed that ground, I told him our family descends from the youngest person to carry a musket there that day. He was just a boy. He stood in a different unit from his father. Father and son, both there. Father and son, both fighting for freedom. Father and son, both fighting for the right of a people to govern themselves.</p><p>Then I looked at my son and said: He was only two years older than you are now.</p><p>That moment has stayed with me. So has another truth. Our roots in the Revolution run through both Massachusetts and Virginia. We also descend from Richard Bland of Virginia, who argued for the rights of the colonies before independence was declared.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-b00000" name="module-b00000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Columnists bug</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h2>Columnists</h2><br><i>In-depth political coverage, sports analysis, entertainment reviews and cultural commentary.</i><br></div> </div><p>So this year, as we mark 250 years since the Declaration, we should ask a simple question: Have we finished what those brave men, women and children started?</p><p>Not yet.</p><p>We elect our mayors by popular vote. We elect our governors by popular vote. But we still do not count every vote equally when we choose our president.</p><p>Try explaining that to a child. It is awkward.</p><p>Because democracy rests on a simple idea: the person with the most votes should win. One person, one vote.</p><p>Today, that is not how presidential politics works in practice. A few swing states get most of the attention. The rest of the country is pushed aside. Millions of Americans in red states and blue states alike are told, in effect, that their votes matter less in the one race that belongs to all of us.</p><p>That is not fairness. That is neglect.</p><p>There is another way.</p><p>It is called the <a class="Link" href="https://www.nationalpopularvote.com/written-explanation" target="_blank" >National Popular Vote Interstate Compact</a>. It does not end the Electoral College. It uses it in the way the Constitution allows. The Constitution gives state legislatures the power to decide how their electoral votes are awarded. Under this plan, states agree to give their electoral votes to the candidate who wins the most votes in all 50 states and the District of Columbia. No constitutional amendment is needed.</p><p>Virginia is now close to joining. The bill has passed both houses of the General Assembly. If Gov. Abigail Spanberger signs it, Virginia would become the 19th jurisdiction in the compact, joining states such as Vermont, Delaware, Maine and Illinois. Virginia is not some side note in this story. It is one of the places where the American idea of self-government first took root. If Virginia joins now, it will not be breaking with the founding. It will be honoring it.</p><p>That is not a radical idea. It is an American one. And it should not belong to one party.</p><p>This is not about helping Republicans. It is not about helping Democrats. It is about trusting the people.</p><p>That idea is not left-wing or right-wing. It is not urban or rural. It is not Black or white.</p><p>It is patriotic.</p><p>Of course, there are still obstacles. More states would need to join. Lawmakers would need to act. Courts may weigh in. None of this is certain.</p><p>But history does not move only when success is guaranteed. It moves when people decide that something is right.</p><p>And what better time than now?</p><p>What better way to honor America’s 250th birthday than by taking one more step toward becoming the democracy we have always said we are?</p><p>The people who stood at Lexington did not risk their lives so future generations could accept minority rule. They did not fight so Americans would shrug when the person with the most votes loses.</p><p>They fought for freedom.</p><p>They fought for self-government.</p><p>They fought for the right of the people to decide their own future.</p><p>It is time to finish what they started.</p><p>Let the people decide.</p><p>Let the majority rule.</p><p><i>Ben Jealous is a professor of practice at the University of Pennsylvania and former president and CEO of the NAACP.</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-b10000" name="module-b10000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Send letters to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. More about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/submitting-opinion-and-letters" >how to submit here</a>.</div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Ben Jealous

entries.title: Piano virtuoso Charles Joseph Smith finally gets his due
entries.summary: <p>Inside his family’s cozy Beverly living room, Charles Joseph Smith takes a seat at the shiny black piano and, without consulting sheet music, begins a commanding performance of Chopin's “Fantaisie-Impromptu.”</p><p>The 55-year-old native Chicagoan is a virtuoso player, completely at ease at the keys. He's also a prolific composer, with an enormous catalog of 600 original works, ranging from heartfelt classical piano solos to a fully formed sci-fi opera featuring aliens from Mars.</p><div class="Form-newsletter-text-wrapper"> <div class="Form-newsletter-title"> Green Room Newsletter </div> <div class="Form-newsletter-submissionSuccess"> <p><b>You’re subscribed!</b></p><p></p><p>Please check your inbox for your confirmation.</p> </div> <div class="Form-newsletter-description">Stay ahead of what’s hitting Chicago stages, galleries, museums and more with Green Room, WBEZ’s weekly arts & culture newsletter!</div> <div class="Form-newsletter-errorMessage"><p>Sorry, there was an error registering your email.</p></div> </div> <form data-ajax-endpoint="https://sfmc-subscribe.wbez.org/v1/subscription" class="Form-newsletter-form" method="POST" > <div class="Form-newsletter-item"> <div class="EmailInput"><label class="EmailInput-label"><span>Email</span> <input class="EmailInput-input" type="email" name="email" placeholder="name@email.com" required aria-required="true"> </label> </div> <button class="Button" type="submit">Sign Up </button> </div> <div class="Form-items-item"> <input type="hidden" name="acquisition_campaign" value="ARTS_Affordance"> </div> <div class="Form-newsletter-disclaimer"> <p>By subscribing, you agree with WBEZ’s <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/terms-of-use" target="_blank" >Terms of Use</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/privacy-policy" target="_blank" >Privacy Policy</a></p> </div> </form> </bsp-form> </div> </div><p>Anyone who has spent time around Chicago's DIY music scene likely recognizes Smith as a mainstay on the dancefloor of venues like The Hideout or Cafe Mustache, where he often sells his homemade cassettes and sometimes performs his own music. But as a Black artist with autism, Smith has struggled to have his work heard beyond that ephemeral world.</p><p>Now, his first widely available release, timed to align with World Autism Awareness Day, marks a major recognition of his talents.</p><p>Chicago-based Sooper Records will drop Smith’s double album on April 3. It features 10 eclectic tracks on “Collected Works,” plus two iterations of his opera, “War of the Martian Ghosts.” The label will also make Smith’s self-published autobiography, “The 88 Keys that Opened Doors,” widely available for the first time.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-270000" name="module-270000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Charles Joseph Smith will perform live on Friday, April 10 at <a class="Link" href="https://dice.fm/partner/tickets/event/oegdq5-charles-joseph-smith-nonnie-parry-10th-apr-rozz-tox-rock-island-tickets?dice_id=8205497&dice_channel=web&dice_tags=organic&dice_campaign=OUTLETProgramme&dice_feature=mio_marketing&_branch_match_id=1307081357473725044&utm_source=web&utm_campaign=OUTLETProgramme&utm_medium=mio_marketing&_branch_referrer=H4sIAAAAAAAAA8soKSkottLXz8nMy9ZLyUxO1UvL1fdPMzI1NEg2TDSxSLGvK0pNSy0qysxLj08qyi8vTi2ydc4oys9NBQA4V3qQOwAAAA%3D%3D" target="_blank" >ROZZ-TOX</a>, 2108 3rd Ave., Rock Island. Tickets from $10. Plus, on Saturday, May 2 at <a class="Link" href="https://elasticarts.org/events/charles-joseph-smith-collected-works-and-war-of-the-martian-ghosts-release-show-w-chelsea-bridge-potions" target="_blank" >Elastic Arts</a>, 3429 W. Diversey Ave. Tickets from $10. He will also appear in conversation at the <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/arts-culture/2026/03/17/padma-lakshmi-veronica-roth-xochitl-gonzalez-michael-pollan-2026-chicago-humanities-fest" target="_blank" >Chicago Humanities Festival</a> on Saturday, April 18.</div> </div><p>Together, the package aims to give Smith his flowers, which many in Chicago’s scrappy, underground music world say is overdue. That scene has provided Smith refuge, he said, to be among “people doing strange things, performance art, strange music, strange vibe and free-form dancing.”</p><p>As Smith finishes the Chopin, his elderly mother, Emma, is near tears. The piece is her favorite. And this moment, when her son’s artistry is on the cusp of wider recognition, feels like the long exhale their household has been waiting for.</p><p>“It's taken a long time to get to a point like this,” Emma Smith said. “Where his talent is going to be available. People will know his music.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-b90000" name="image-b90000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ef0945e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fce%2F2f%2F1182a65243beaa45a4eca46e72d9%2Fsmithmusic-260331-1.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/eff1063/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fce%2F2f%2F1182a65243beaa45a4eca46e72d9%2Fsmithmusic-260331-1.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/131e566/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fce%2F2f%2F1182a65243beaa45a4eca46e72d9%2Fsmithmusic-260331-1.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Charles Joseph Smith, whose first album, “Collected Works and War of the Martian Ghosts,” is being released by Sooper Records, smiles in his home in Beverly, Tuesday, March 24, 2026. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/131e566/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fce%2F2f%2F1182a65243beaa45a4eca46e72d9%2Fsmithmusic-260331-1.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/449cea0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fce%2F2f%2F1182a65243beaa45a4eca46e72d9%2Fsmithmusic-260331-1.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/131e566/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fce%2F2f%2F1182a65243beaa45a4eca46e72d9%2Fsmithmusic-260331-1.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>“I had a Mozart mentality. I realized if Mozart could do this, maybe I could do that,” Smith said. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><b>A young boy with something to say</b></h3><p>Charles Joseph Smith was born in 1970 on Chicago's South Side. As a young child, he did not speak for several years. His worried parents took him to a series of doctors, who eventually concluded that Smith was selectively mute.</p><p>In an era when autism was understudied and widely stigmatized, it took years for the Smith family to fully understand and embrace their son’s diagnosis: autism spectrum disorder. But in that murkiness, Smith found clarity in music.</p><p>As an 8-year-old, Smith tagged along to his older brother’s piano lesson. Despite never having touched the keys, the younger Smith sibling began to play with prodigious precision. Quickly surpassing his brother, Smith grew confident in his abilities.</p><p>“I had a Mozart mentality. I realized if Mozart could do this, maybe I could do that,” said Smith, who is dressed on this day in a purple cable knit sweater, thick rim glasses, jeans and a well-worn pair of loafers.</p><p>His mother recalled how, as her son was growing up, well-meaning psychologists would tell her and her husband, a late mathematician, that their son “might be able to make some beads or something like that.”</p><p>“My husband and I were saying, we're going to all these parents meetings, counseling sessions and you mean, all that's going to happen is he is going to be making beads? We could feel that he had something more than that in him,” Emma Smith said.</p><p>“I think the music was the key to bringing him out of wherever his mind was. And it made him feel, like <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2026/03/06/jesse-jackson-chicago-service-obama-biden-clinton-kamala-harris" target="_blank" ><u>Rev. Jesse Jackson said,</u></a> like he was somebody.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-540000" name="image-540000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/540c2e5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1875x1052+0+724/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4d%2F4c%2F00114216430dbda62c110409d1af%2Fhi-res-image-charles-joseph-smith-by-dennis-larance-2-2.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2c2f755/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1875x1052+0+724/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4d%2F4c%2F00114216430dbda62c110409d1af%2Fhi-res-image-charles-joseph-smith-by-dennis-larance-2-2.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/873754e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1875x1052+0+724/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4d%2F4c%2F00114216430dbda62c110409d1af%2Fhi-res-image-charles-joseph-smith-by-dennis-larance-2-2.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Charles Joseph Smith" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/873754e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1875x1052+0+724/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4d%2F4c%2F00114216430dbda62c110409d1af%2Fhi-res-image-charles-joseph-smith-by-dennis-larance-2-2.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f78641e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1875x1052+0+724/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4d%2F4c%2F00114216430dbda62c110409d1af%2Fhi-res-image-charles-joseph-smith-by-dennis-larance-2-2.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/873754e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1875x1052+0+724/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4d%2F4c%2F00114216430dbda62c110409d1af%2Fhi-res-image-charles-joseph-smith-by-dennis-larance-2-2.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Smith’s sound mirrors both what you may hear in storied classical music halls, but also tiny dive bars or free basement shows.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Courtesy of Dennis Larance</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>As a teenager, Smith finally saw autism represented in the broader culture. Temple Grandin’s 1986 memoir about living with autism and Dustin Hoffman’s 1988 performance in “Rain Man” helped him feel seen.</p><p>“I now realized people are no longer keeping it secret,” Smith said. “They just realized it's pervasive, it's everywhere. At that point, I didn't realize my gift of composing and piano was likely due to Asperger's [a form of autism].”</p><p></p><h3><b>Finding his groove</b></h3><p>As the album’s extensive liner notes point out, Smith exists somewhere between a classically trained virtuoso and an experimental outsider artist. His sound mirrors both what you may hear in storied classical music halls, but also tiny dive bars or free basement shows.</p><p>After college at Roosevelt University, Smith earned a doctorate in music from the University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign. During his studies, he performed as a concert pianist in Italy, Germany, France and Hungary. In 2000, Smith earned an honorable mention from the French Piano Institute in Paris. But outside the structure of school, he found it hard to sustain momentum as a professional musician.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe width="200" height="150" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/okgalofOpPs?feature=oembed" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen title="Love Theme (1996)"></iframe> </div> </div></div><p>Back home in Chicago, he found work as an accompanist for a local vocal coach at the Fine Arts Building and in South Side churches. But he also threw himself into his own body of work. In addition to the classical giants — Mozart, Beethoven, Chopin — Smith is inspired by the likes of Billy Joel, Paul Simon, Leontyne Price and his first musical love, Debby Boone’s “You Light Up My Life.”</p><p>“It lifts you, it makes you feel like you can get through even the worst tribulation,” Smith said.</p><p>All that inspiration — from classical to soaring pop ballads — is reflected on “Collected Works,” on which each track is a surprising, delightful departure from the one before it. None of it seems to go together, yet somehow it does, and it’s all the product of a singular, prolific mind.</p><p>Where “Love Theme” is a traditional classical piano piece, the punchy “Beats for Katie S.” features whistles and a spinningly fast pace. Two rare songs showcase Smith’s voice, “My Days Are Not Over For Me” and the operatic “The Five Louises.”</p><p>Smith’s giant catalog was pared down to these 10 tracks by Glenn Curran, who cofounded Sooper in 2016, alongside fellow musicians <a class="Link" href="https://vocalo.org/nnamdi-npr-music" target="_blank" ><u>Nnamdi Ogbonnaya</u></a> and <a class="Link" href="https://vocalo.org/sen-morimoto-diagnosis-bekoe-interview23" target="_blank" ><u>Sen Morimoto</u></a>. In 2024, the label purchased 64 unique CDs of Smith’s music spanning across 30 years — and Curran began curating, eventually licensing the songs that appear on the album.</p><p>“I just started to take the tracks that really moved me and that also represented all of the different things that Charles could do, and then continued to narrow it down from there,” Curran said. “Which is kind of an impossible task, but I did my best.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-660000" name="image-660000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" width="490" height="275" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Charles Joseph Smith - Collected Works and War of the Martian Ghosts" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c7670b5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+658/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F59%2F0d%2F73168c084cd19692be0c223c9870%2Ffront-cover-charles-joseph-smith-collected-works-and-war-of-the-martian-ghosts-1.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/aea9994/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+658/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F59%2F0d%2F73168c084cd19692be0c223c9870%2Ffront-cover-charles-joseph-smith-collected-works-and-war-of-the-martian-ghosts-1.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c7670b5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+658/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F59%2F0d%2F73168c084cd19692be0c223c9870%2Ffront-cover-charles-joseph-smith-collected-works-and-war-of-the-martian-ghosts-1.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Smith’s giant catalog was pared down to 10 tracks on “Collected Works” by Glenn Curran, who cofounded Sooper in 2016, alongside fellow musicians Nnamdi Ogbonnaya and Sen Morimoto.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Courtesy of Charles Joseph Smith</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The new album is the second time Curran and the Sooper team have worked with Smith. The first was in 2017, when the label invited Smith to record some of his piano works in the studio. After recording a couple dozen pieces in just one or two takes, Smith unexpectedly launched into “War of the Martian Ghosts,” wowing the recording team with a “thundering, atonal, through-composed work of solo piano.”</p><p>After performing the work live at The Hideout for a sold-out crowd, Smith developed the piano piece into a full sci-fi opera during the COVID-19 pandemic, creating a formal score and script. Smith hopes someday the opera will come to life as a fully staged production.</p><p>The Smith project is Sooper’s first retrospective. Presented in the handmade spirit in which it was created, listeners can hear pops, static and crackles.</p><p>“I wanted to present the archive of the artist as I found it,” Curran said. “That was the goal. I was looking at it as an archival re-release, or a retrospective collection of this artist and his really massive body of work.”</p><p>It’s a look back, sure. But, it's also an introduction to a Chicago musician who many once assumed had little to say. </p><p><i>Courtney Kueppers is an arts and culture reporter at WBEZ.</i></p>
entries.author: Courtney Kueppers | WBEZ

entries.title: HIV, AIDS infections up in Chicago after years of decline as cases rise among Latinos
entries.summary: <p>Ricardo Jiménez feels like he’s going crazy.</p><p>The leader of public health initiatives at Chicago’s Puerto Rican Cultural Center is desperately trying to find a way to keep his HIV prevention programs afloat. But amid funding cuts and a backlash against diversity and equity efforts, he worries his Humboldt Park-based health programs could be wiped out by this summer unless new funding is secured.</p><p>“People will lose their jobs and that probably will include me, too,” he said.</p><p>It couldn’t come at a more challenging time.</p><p>HIV infections are on the rise in Chicago after about two decades of decline, growing 29% between 2022 and 2024. AIDS cases are also up slightly. In 2024, there were 818 new HIV cases — and nearly half were among Latinos, the population Jiménez focuses on. That increase is particularly significant, marking the first time the racial and ethnic group accounted for most of the new diagnoses, said John Peller, CEO of the AIDS Foundation Chicago.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-120000" name="html-embed-module-120000"></a><div class="flourish-embed flourish-chart" data-src="visualisation/28234189"><script src="https://public.flourish.studio/resources/embed.js"></script><noscript><img src="https://public.flourish.studio/visualisation/28234189/thumbnail" width="100%" alt="chart visualization" /></noscript></div> </div> </div><p>Chicago health officials say some early clues behind the increase among Latinos include a change in migration patterns which possibly included people who hadn’t been tested or maintained care, along with ripple effects of the COVID-19 pandemic that could have interrupted HIV care.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-c10000" name="html-embed-module-c10000"></a><div class="flourish-embed flourish-chart" data-src="visualisation/28213472"><script src="https://public.flourish.studio/resources/embed.js"></script><noscript><img src="https://public.flourish.studio/visualisation/28213472/thumbnail" width="100%" alt="visualization" /></noscript></div> </div> </div><p>The change comes amid two contradictory trends: Testing and treatment for HIV has advanced so much that it allows people with the virus who are treated to live relatively normal lives and, critically, not transmit the virus to anyone else. But those advancements are at risk as funding for treatment and prevention for communities most at risk are under attack.</p><p>“What we’re seeing right now is really setting us back in the trajectory of the HIV epidemic and it shouldn’t be that way,” Peller said. “It’s going to ultimately cost taxpayers and society more money to take care of people who are living with HIV, who may come down with an AIDS diagnosis, [and] end up in the hospital then it would be to prevent HIV in the first place.”</p><h3>HIV cases among Latinos grow post-pandemic </h3><p>The number of Latinos living with HIV or AIDS in Chicago grew nearly 17% from 2020 to 2024 while it dropped among the white and Black population during that period, according to the most recent city data. And in 2024, there was an increase in people with a late diagnosis of HIV/AIDS — they were diagnosed with HIV and then AIDS within 12 months — and half of those 156 people were Latinos.</p><p>Chicago Department of Public Health Commissioner Dr. Olusimbo Ige said officials are looking into possible explanations, including a change in migration from 2022 to 2024.</p><p>During that time, more than 51,000 newly arrived immigrants, many who identify as Latino, resettled in Chicago. They were bused here and other Democrat-led cities by Republican Texas Gov. Greg Abbott as a political ploy to critique former President Joe Biden’s immigration policies. Some new cases could be among that population, which was getting tested for the first time, Ige said. People living in city-run shelters underwent medical screenings and testing.</p><p>“We want people to know that HIV is not a disease of one community or another. It is an area where we need everyone to be protected,” Ige said in an interview with the Chicago Sun-Times. “And we need to change our strategies to be community oriented and culturally appropriate, but we don’t want any community to be stigmatized.”</p><p>The increase could also be fallout of the COVID-19 pandemic. People delayed medical care because of a change to insurance or because they were afraid of falling sick with the virus, Ige said.</p><p>“Once that momentum shifts, it is hard to regain it,” Ige said. “It’s convincing people that PrEP is still important, staying connected to your care is still important, keeping your medical appointment is still important.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-580000" name="image-580000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source
