2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ab2f27f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fda%2Fcf%2F10037e9c4f4b85dd9dee838a7d9c%2Fclinics-053124-04.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Dr. Olusimbo Ige, commissioner of the Chicago Department of Public Health, speaking in at a press conference." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ab2f27f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fda%2Fcf%2F10037e9c4f4b85dd9dee838a7d9c%2Fclinics-053124-04.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c34a81a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fda%2Fcf%2F10037e9c4f4b85dd9dee838a7d9c%2Fclinics-053124-04.JPG 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ab2f27f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fda%2Fcf%2F10037e9c4f4b85dd9dee838a7d9c%2Fclinics-053124-04.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Dr. Olusimbo Ige, commissioner of the Chicago Department of Public Health, says health officials are looking into what might be behind the increase in HIV cases in Chicago. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Zubaer Khan/Sun-Times file</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>PrEP is a medication that prevents HIV infection. While there is not a cure for HIV, medication, called antiretroviral therapy, can make it undetectable so an individual cannot transmit it, Peller said.</p><p>But if a person stops taking their antiviral therapy because their care is disrupted, the likelihood of transmitting HIV increases and it could result in more complex and costly treatments to get back to an undetectable viral load, Peller said.</p><p>To reach people at risk, the Puerto Rican Cultural Center went to nontraditional settings, like nightclubs and bars with special Latino music nights to offer HIV testing. It was also an avenue to inform people about medications and other resources, said Tichike Tumalan, a program coordinator. But without funding, they won’t be able to do that outreach.</p><p>“We are taking a couple steps back,” Tumalan said. “But also understanding our trans experience and also being Latino — I think we’ve always been in a position of uncertainty.”</p><p>The gap in accessing medications, in particular PreP, remains wide for Black and Latino populations, said Dr. Andrew Crone, an infectious disease specialist at Chicago-based Howard Brown Health. Cost could be one barrier, Crone said. He noted that at nearly 46% of all people living with HIV, Black patients still have the largest share of all cases and a rate disproportionate to their overall population. That rate dropped from nearly 49% in 2020 as cases dropped slightly and the number of Latino cases rose. Black residents also had the highest percentage of new AIDS cases in 2024.</p><p>There are programs that provide medication for people without health insurance or who are living in the U.S. without legal status, Crone said. Still, people are increasingly hesitant to share information with the government about their immigration or HIV status.</p><p>“We’re in a situation where people who previously knew they had access to medication and care are afraid to seek it because they are afraid of something happening to them,” Crone said.</p><p></p><h3>‘Perfect storm’ amid funding crunch </h3><p>Reaching the community most in need takes time and money. But funding for public health remains in limbo.</p><p>In February, the Trump administration announced millions of cuts to public health programs, including HIV prevention. In Illinois, the grants were restored, but funding only lasts through June, and it’s unlikely to be extended, Peller said. Those funds trickled down to the Puerto Rican Cultural Center, which is not expecting the funds to be renewed for another year.</p><p>Across the country, states facing tightened budgets are limiting access to the AIDS Drug Assistance Program, which provides HIV treatment to individuals through a combination of federal, state and local funds, according to an analysis from KFF, a nonprofit health policy research and news organization. While the program hasn’t been limited in Illinois, Peller said the AIDS Foundation Chicago, along with other advocacy groups, are pushing the state Legislature to increase funding for HIV programs.</p><p>“It’s really a perfect storm of demand and need right now for state HIV programs,” Peller said. “We are having the increasing cost from the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2025/12/18/obamacare-aca-tax-credits-expiring-premiums-congress-january-spike-health-care-insurance" target="_blank" ><u>[Affordable Care Act] tax credits expiring</u></a>, we have <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2026/01/06/medicaid-unchanged-for-now-cook-county-officials-spike-in-misinformation-and-confusion" target="_blank" ><u>Medicaid cuts</u></a> coming, and we have an increase in the number of people living with HIV in Chicago.”</p><p>Gov. JB Pritzker’s office said it was monitoring legislation, but noted that any bill has to be carefully reviewed "to understand the fiscal impact," and pointed out that the Illinois Department of Public Health is developing a five-year plan to address HIV prevention and care.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-d90000" name="module-d90000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><b><u>Where to find help</u></b><br><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="margin-top:0;margin-bottom:0;padding-inline-start:48px;" id="rte-0e5d5010-2bd9-11f1-b93e-6b53e9a7fa54"><li><b>Spanish-language resources:</b> Visit <a class="Link" href="https://hivhub.org/es/" target="_blank" ><u>https://hivhub.org/es/</u></a> for resources from the AIDS Foundation Chicago and the Center on Halsted. </li><li><b>HIV prevention services</b>: To reach the Puerto Rican Cultural Center, call (773) 278-6737. </li><li><b>Find community:</b> CALOR, 3201 W. North Ave., is hosting open houses on April 2 and and April 4 for an empowering circle for trans women, transfeminine people and those questioning. For more details visit <a class="Link" href="http://instagram.com/calorchicago" target="_blank" ><u>instagram.com/calorchicago</u></a>. </li></ul></div> </div><p>Jiménez had hoped city grants would keep their programs running. By March, the organization received another grant denial and believed they were being zeroed out of city funding. They gathered in the heart of Humboldt Park with other community groups to call on state officials to dip into emergency funds to continue HIV testing and medications.</p><p>“We are asking for answers, we are asking for dialogue and most importantly we are asking that communities we serve are not abandoned,” said Juan Calderón, a leader at the Puerto Rican Cultural Center, during a March news conference.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-360000" name="image-360000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e5679cb/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7270x4080+0+384/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F67%2F99%2Ff421656e402cb490755061c8eb30%2Fhivstat-260321-011.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e1917b5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7270x4080+0+384/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F67%2F99%2Ff421656e402cb490755061c8eb30%2Fhivstat-260321-011.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/65e6732/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7270x4080+0+384/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F67%2F99%2Ff421656e402cb490755061c8eb30%2Fhivstat-260321-011.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="A pro-transgender display at the Puerto Rican Cultural Center in Humboldt Park in Chicago." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/65e6732/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7270x4080+0+384/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F67%2F99%2Ff421656e402cb490755061c8eb30%2Fhivstat-260321-011.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/151f547/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7270x4080+0+384/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F67%2F99%2Ff421656e402cb490755061c8eb30%2Fhivstat-260321-011.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/65e6732/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7270x4080+0+384/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F67%2F99%2Ff421656e402cb490755061c8eb30%2Fhivstat-260321-011.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The Puerto Rican Cultural Center historically has done outreach at nightclubs and bars to find people at risk, to offer HIV testing and let them know about medications and other resources. But without funding, that will have to stop.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The Chicago health department, which says it has only received partial federal funding, confirmed it denied three applications from the center, but it would provide some funding for mental health and emergency housing programs.</p><p>Ige, from the city’s health department, said it is looking for partners that match the community areas that saw HIV cases rise last year that are also culturally competent.</p><p>Long-term change to address how Latinos see HIV will also need to include community-driven services like support groups, said Alfredo Flores, associate director of operations at CALOR, a Chicago-based group that provides Spanish-language HIV services.</p><p>He says prevention has mostly focused on HIV care and PrEP, “and it hasn’t really been on building sustainable systems to encourage education, to encourage community-building and to encourage community education.”</p><p>For Esmeralda Lozano, an HIV tester and counselor for the Puerto Rican Cultural Center, the funding uncertainty is scary even for her, as she worries about how she will continue to access PrEP and the health care she needs as a trans woman. But that won’t stop her from trying to pass on a positive message to those she interacts with, stressing to people that they are worthy of health care.</p><p>“We’re here to empower ourselves and just embrace us,” Lozano said.</p>
entries.author: Elvia Malagón

entries.title: Jackson Park’s 'Gold Lady' could look like a million again, thanks to a planned restoration
entries.summary: <p>On just the right morning, when things are crisp and clear enough, the sun rises over the lake and lights up the gilded two-story Statue of the Republic in Jackson Park.</p><p>And the Gold Lady — as she's informally known — proudly faces that rising sun as she's done since 1918, draped in a tunic, crowned with a laurel and with her arms aloft. She holds an eagle-topped globe in her right hand and a staff bearing the word “Liberty” in her left.</p><p>But the spectacle, though still impressive, has been losing its luster in recent years. The gold leaf is growing dull and some of it has flaked away enough to expose the bronze underneath.</p><p>The work still shines, but not like it once did.</p><p>The Chicago Park District is now making plans to repair and regild the Gold Lady starting in May. The $1 million job includes repairing and removing the 24-foot statue's worn and flaking gold leaf and applying a new layer of the micro-thin substance over the entire work.</p><p>"It's beautiful now ... but once the gilding is on, I think people will be stunned by the transformation," Chicago Park District Director of Historic Assets Andrew Schneider said.</p><p>So here's to The Republic, for which it stands — right there at 63rd Street and Hayes Drive traffic circle. The statue was last regilded 33 years ago, so it's about time.</p><p>And it's good to see one of Jackson Park's jewels — one that lies beyond the grounds of Obamaland on the north end of the park — get some long-needed attention.</p><h3>‘Our Statue of Liberty’</h3><p>Designed by sculptor Daniel Chester French, the sculpture is a scaled-down replica of the 65-foot gilded Statue of the Republic that sat in a basin facing west toward the neo-classical Court of Honor and its high-domed Administration Building at the 1893 World's Columbian Exposition, held in Jackson Park.</p><p>"The 1893 World's Fair was one of the great fairs of the world," Chicago author and historian Rolf Achilles said.</p><p>"It not only brought tens of thousands of people to Chicago, but it really helped put Chicago on the map and brought people to see the new architecture in Chicago," Achilles said.</p><p>The statue was designed to celebrate the American ideals of liberty and its republic form of governance.</p><p>"That was the whole concept," Achilles said. "The sun was hitting America, and America was aglow at the time. Even if it was a white man's concept, it was still a concept and it worked, in part. And that made her a centerpiece of what people went to the fair and took home with them as visual."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-6e0000" name="image-6e0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6c337ff/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4443x2494+0+305/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F88%2Fa5%2Fc811f642e7f0aa05c6f87f07013d%2Flooking-west-from-peristyle-court-of-honor-and-grand-basin-1893.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f6c90ed/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4443x2494+0+305/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F88%2Fa5%2Fc811f642e7f0aa05c6f87f07013d%2Flooking-west-from-peristyle-court-of-honor-and-grand-basin-1893.jpg width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3933ec9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4443x2494+0+305/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F88%2Fa5%2Fc811f642e7f0aa05c6f87f07013d%2Flooking-west-from-peristyle-court-of-honor-and-grand-basin-1893.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="A black-and-white image of the original Statue of the Republic from the 1893 World’s Fair." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3933ec9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4443x2494+0+305/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F88%2Fa5%2Fc811f642e7f0aa05c6f87f07013d%2Flooking-west-from-peristyle-court-of-honor-and-grand-basin-1893.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f31e057/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4443x2494+0+305/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F88%2Fa5%2Fc811f642e7f0aa05c6f87f07013d%2Flooking-west-from-peristyle-court-of-honor-and-grand-basin-1893.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3933ec9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4443x2494+0+305/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F88%2Fa5%2Fc811f642e7f0aa05c6f87f07013d%2Flooking-west-from-peristyle-court-of-honor-and-grand-basin-1893.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A view of the original Statue of the Republic from the 1893 World’s Fair.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Sun-Times file</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>But the first Gold Lady and all the fair buildings — with the exception of the Palace of Fine Arts, which later became the Museum of Science and Industry — were temporary plaster structures that were wrecked and removed after the exposition.</p><p>Park officials got rid of the Gold Lady by burning her down.</p><p>A quarter century after the fair and five years before creating the Sitting Lincoln sculpture at Washington, D.C.,'s Lincoln Memorial, French returned to Jackson Park to create the current Statue of the Republic.</p><p>The new statue would commemorate the exposition's 25th anniversary and the century that had passed since Illinois won statehood.</p><p>The sculpture was placed on the old site of the fair's Administration Building and faces east. Architect Henry Bacon, who oversaw the design and engineering of the Lincoln Memorial, created the 10-foot base.</p><p>"It strikes me as our own Statue of Liberty," Hyde Park resident and parks advocate Bronwyn Nichols Lodato said. "The regilding allows for there to be a reassessment of what that statue means and what that history means for the South Side ... and to rethink our place in the world ... what it means to be an American ... in the midst of all that's happening."</p><h3>A ‘great treasure’</h3><p>Workers will remove all of the existing gold leaf then laser clean and repair the entire sculpture. Then they'll put on an epoxy primer, a base layer, a polymer base — both gold-colored — and an adhesive.</p><p>Next comes the 2 millimeter thick gold leaf, somewhere between 12,000 to 15,000 pieces, that will be applied by hand, almost like laying on the fixings of a submarine sandwich.</p><p>The Art Institute of Chicago's 121-year-old B.F. Ferguson Monument Fund is kicking in $140,000 toward purchasing the gold leaf, a spokesperson for the museum said.</p><p>The job could take two to three months to complete. The statue will be encapsulated during the work.</p><p>Schneider said the Park District is looking to restore the Gold Lady's base in the next phase of work — bringing the total price to at least $2.3 million, he said. The agency is also considering adding lighting to the statue at some point.</p><p>And here's hoping they rethink the traffic circle plaza on which the statue sits, which has no seating where parks patrons can rest and enjoy the work.</p><p>The restoration comes three months after the agency announced plans to restore a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/architecture-design/2026/01/09/obama-presidential-center-jackson-park-pavilion" >historic open air pavilion</a> at the southern end of the park.</p><p>Schneider said the district is looking at the park's other neglected features, such as the Clarence Darrow Memorial Bridge, located just south of the Griffin Museum of Science and Industry, that has been closed to pedestrians for safety reason by the Chicago Department of Transportation since 2013.</p><p>"Jackson Park is one of the great, one of the great treasures of our city, and it contains within it many great treasures," Schneider said. "So we're pleased to show this commitment to the people of the city Chicago."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a entries.author: Lee Bey

entries.title: Three Chicago restaurants are 2026 James Beard Award finalists
entries.summary: <p>Three Chicago restaurants are finalists for this year’s prestigious James Beard Awards. And, the food industry’s highest honor announced Tuesday that the ceremony will remain in Chicago at least through 2028 — a one-year extension from what had been previously announced. </p><p>Bailey Sullivan, of West Loop’s Monteverde Restaurant & Pastificio, is a finalist in the emerging chef category. Plus, Norman Fenton of Cariño in Uptown and Jacob Potashnick of Feld in Ukrainian Village are among this year’s finalists in the best chef Great Lakes category, the James Beard Foundation announced Tuesday from The Wellsley, a private event space in Chicago’s River North, owned by the Boka Restaurant Group.</p><p>“Because it’s a time of real challenge and uncertainty, it's important to say this with certainty: Independent restaurants are not just businesses, you are the very fabric of American culture,” said Clare Reichenbach, CEO of the Beard Foundation. “You fuel economies and ecosystems. You tell the story of where we come from, who we are today and who we are becoming.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Feld at 2018 W. Chicago Ave. in Ukrainian Village</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The Beards, often dubbed “the Oscars of the food world,” have been hosted in Chicago since 2015, after moving from New York. In 2018, former <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2018/12/10/18465924/james-beard-awards-to-stay-in-chicago-through-2027" ><u>Mayor Rahm Emanuel spearheaded</u></a> an effort to keep the ceremony in Chicago an additional nine years — through 2027. With that deadline now approaching, the foundation and Choose Chicago announced an additional extension on the Beard’s time in Chicago, although, this time, by just one year. Still, city and state leaders heralded it as a win.</p><p>“This extension is a recognition of Chicago’s extraordinary culinary community and the chefs, restaurateurs, and hospitality professionals who make our city one of the great food destinations in the world,” Kristen Reynolds, Choose’s president and CEO, said in a statement.</p><p>The Beards previously named 23 Chicago restaurants among <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/food-drink/2026/01/21/james-beard-chicago-semifinalists-2026-food-restaurants-chef-awards-best" target="_blank" ><u>this year’s semifinalists</u></a>.</p><p>Last year, <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/food-drink/2025/04/02/james-beard-awards-2025-finalists-chicago-restaurants-chefs" target="_blank" ><u>five Chicago restaurants</u></a> were Beard finalists, while two — <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/food-drink/2025/06/16/james-beard-awards-2025-winners-chicago" target="_blank" ><u>Kumiko and Oriole</u></a> — took home medals. South Side staple <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/food-and-restaurants/2025/02/27/lems-bar-b-q-named-americas-classic-by-james-beard-awards-foundation" ><u>Lem’s Bar-B-Q</u></a> also earned a 2025 honor as an “America’s Classics” awardee.</p><p>This year’s James Beard Awards will be held on June 15 at Chicago’s Lyric Opera House.</p><h3>Here are the Chicago chefs and restaurants named 2026 James Beard Award finalists:</h3><p><b>Emerging Chef </b><br></p><ul id="rte-16964a20-2d14-11f1-b3c6-33575cbcfbb6"><li>Bailey Sullivan, Monteverde Restaurant & Pastificio</li></ul><p><b>Best Chef: Great Lakes (IL, IN, MI, OH) </b><br></p><ul id="rte-16964a21-2d14-11f1-b3c6-33575cbcfbb6"><li>Norman Fenton, Cariño</li><li>Jacob Potashnick, Feld</li></ul><p><i>Courtney Kueppers is an arts and culture reporter at WBEZ. </i></p>
entries.author: Courtney Kueppers | WBEZ

entries.title: Waves of potentially severe storms may bring 'golf ball sized hail' to Chicago area
entries.summary: <p>"Waves" of potentially severe thunderstorms are approaching the Chicago area as a moving cold front will cause temperatures to plummet more than 20 degrees Tuesday. </p><p>The storms bring a slight chance for flooding and could produce golf ball sized hail and "damaging" winds up to 60 mph in the southwest suburbs, according to the National Weather Service. A severe thunderstorm watch is in effect until 5:30 p.m. for 13 counties in Illinois including Cook, DuPage and Kane counties, the NWS said.</p><p>A ground stop was issued at O'Hare International Airport around 8:30 a.m., according to the Federal Aviation Administration. At least 10 flights were canceled and over 180 more were delayed as of 9:50 a.m., according to <a class="Link" href="https://www.flightaware.com/live/cancelled/today/KORD" target="_blank" >FlightAware. </a></p><p>Throughout the day, the Chicago area was expected to see temperatures in the high 60s to low 70s before plummeting down into the 50s by Tuesday night. </p><p>"There's just a cold front that's dropping through the area, and on the north side of that front there are much colder temperatures than there are on the south side of the front," said Rafal Ogorek, a meteorologist with the weather service. "We're currently south of the front, so that's why we're observing the warmer temperatures right now. But as the front moves through the area, those colder temperatures will move into the Chicago metro area." </p><p>A separate storm system will bring periodic rain and thunderstorms throughout the week starting Wednesday, according to Ogorek. </p><p>"That'll bring a renewed chance for showers and thunderstorms in the area." </p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Passover 2026 — remembering one difficult time in another difficult time
entries.summary: <p>Passover <i>and</i> April Fool's. On the same day! The possibilities are endless. I feel compelled to greet our guests at Wednesday night's Seder with a hearty, "Welcome! Let's eat!"</p><p>Not laughing? As with all jokes, it's only funny if you know the set-up: Seder means "order" in Hebrew, and the meal only comes after a protracted span of praying and storytelling. Some years we don't eat until 9 p.m.</p><p>Makes no sense, right? Then you're probably not Jewish, like 97.5% of Americans. Jews are a shrinking shard. Rather than control the world, we can't even control our own children, who wander off, as kids will.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-c10000" name="module-c10000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Opinion bug</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h2>Opinion</h2></div> </div><p>My wife, in her infinite wisdom, introduced a new Seder tradition: preliminary soup. We say a few throat-clearing prayers, and then her excellent, cannonball-dense, matzo ball soup is served, to fortify participants for the hour or two until the festive meal proper begins, the exact time being a tug-of-war between grey-bearded traditionalists and the younger generation, who want to eat and race back to their real lives.</p><p>I suppose the strictly religious might view early broth as the kind of canonical slippage that leads to Christmas trees and, eventually, even fewer Jews. I consider it kindness toward hungry relatives who have consented — heck, some traveled long distances — to endure this dusty rigmarole in return for a hearty meal, eventually, and all the wine they can hold.</p><p>My late colleague Roger Ebert once said that his entire political view can be summed up by "kindness." I'd like to extend that to religious orientation — if your religion doesn't prompt you to be kind, first and foremost, then it's just another tool for oppression, like the others. All religions are the same in that regard, or as I've said before: religion is a hammer: you can use it to build a house, or to hit somebody in the head. Same hammer.</p><p>Focusing on cosmetic differences seems so strange to me. "Oh, you've got an Estwing? Well, MY hammer is a Stanley. I believe the wooden handle absorbs shock better..."</p><p>Thus fortified, antisemitism rolls off me. All bigotry is ignorance married to fear. How much mental energy should be spent getting upset that the person viewing life through a keyhole caught sight of you? Someone who has lapped up the vile poison trickling through gutters for a thousand years now wants to upchuck a bit on my shoes. How hurt am I supposed to be? "Oh boo, frickin' hoo. The kneejerk hater who bought a load of idiotic bilge doesn't like me..."</p><p>Maybe I'm hardened, as a newspaper columnist who hears from haters daily. I don't want to underestimate the scary turn the country has taken after the Oct. 7, 2023 attacks, and the current war in Iran, in lockstep with our good buddy, Israel. The latest twist on antisemitism — that Israel is a monstrous evil that should have never existed in the first place and must be stamped out by force — is certainly frightening, for its popularity, though it's really just a new set of steps to a very old dance, the classic Jews Don't Belong Here Polka. Don't know the words? You can hum along: "<i>Life ... would be great ... but we've got these Jews here ... infesting ... INSERT LOCATION ... where they don't belong ... and we'd all ... be so much happier... if only they'd go live in ... INSERT SOME SPOT FAR AWAY.... " </i></p><p>See why we have a big dinner? If you had escaped arrows fired at you for the past 3,000 years, from Nebuchadnezzar to the current crop of incoming freshmen, you might be in a festive mood too. I really like Passover, and could list a dozen reasons why. Space dwindles, so I'll stick with my top four: 1) Dinner with family; 2) Special food; 3) Expressions of gratitude and 4) a great story.</p><p>Underline "gratitude." We're all slaves to something, struggling to break free. One year, I read excerpts from John Berryman's <a class="Link" href="https://www.poetryfoundation.org/poems/48948/eleven-addresses-to-the-lord" target="_blank" >“Eleven Addresses to the Lord,”</a> which begins,</p><p><i>Master of beauty, craftsman of the snowflake,</i><br><i>inimitable contriver,</i><br><i>endower of Earth so gorgeous & different from the boring Moon,</i><br><i>thank you for such as it is my gift. </i></p><p>Love "boring moon." Though my favorite lines are these:</p><p><i>Fearful I peer upon the mountain path</i><br><i>where once Your shadow passed. Limner of the clouds</i><br><i>... I am afraid,</i><br><i>I never until now confessed.</i><br><i>I fell back in love with you, Father, for two reasons:</i><br><i>You were good to me, & a delicious author.</i></p><p>See why Seders take so long? Anyway, Happy Passover to those who celebrate. And to those who hate... maybe if you applied some of that incredible energy spent looking outward and condemning others to looking inward and improving yourself, well, couldn't hurt.</p>
entries.author: Neil Steinberg

entries.title: New era for Chicago Public Schools
entries.summary: <h3>Good morning, Chicago. ✶</h3><p>🔎 <b>Below</b>: Confirmed almost unanimously, Chicago Public Schools' new CEO Macquline King received a standing ovation Monday from the Board of Education. AIDS cases are also up slightly. In 2024, there were 818 new HIV cases — and nearly half were among Latinos, the first time the racial and ethnic group accounted for most new diagnoses, AIDS Foundation Chicago CEO John Peller said.</p><p><b>How we got here</b>: Chicago health officials say one early clue behind the increase among Latinos is a change in migration patterns, which possibly include people who hadn’t been tested or maintained care. Ripple effects of the COVID-19 pandemic also could have interrupted HIV care.</p><p><b>Key context</b>: The change comes amid two contradictory trends. Testing and treatment for HIV have advanced so much that it allows people with the virus who are treated to live relatively normal lives and, critically, not transmit the virus to anyone else. But those advancements are at risk as funding for treatment and prevention is threatened.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wtuhyil-l-k/" target="_blank" ><b>READ href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wtuhyil-l-p/" target="_blank" >Climate change is fueling surge in Illinois home insurance premiums</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wtuhyil-l-x/" target="_blank" >Juanpablo Ramirez-Franco</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wtuhyil-l-m/" target="_blank" >Jake Bittle</a></h5><p><b>Rising costs</b>: Illinois home insurance premiums are going up, and climate change-caused severe weather is partly to blame. Average U.S. home insurance rates climbed 12% last year and are projected to surge another 4% in 2026, according to new nationwide data from the insurance price tracker Insurify. Illinois is expected to top the national average with a 5% increase, leaving policyholders statewide paying an average $3,559 premium per year.</p><p><b>Climate change</b>: There are a lot of factors behind rising insurance bills. Companies consider the value of a home, the cost of materials that would be needed to rebuild it, even the homeowner's credit score. But according to Insurify’s Matt Brannon, one primary culprit is the rising toll of extreme weather as the planet warms.</p><p><a class="Link" And the Gold Lady — as she’s informally known — proudly faces that rising sun as she’s done since 1918, draped in a tunic, crowned with a laurel and with her arms aloft. She holds an eagle-topped globe in her right hand and a staff bearing the word "Liberty" in her left.</p><p><b>At issue</b>: The spectacle, though impressive, has been losing its luster in recent years. The gold leaf is growing dull and some of it has flaked away enough to expose the bronze underneath. The work still shines, but not like it once did.</p><p><b>$1M makeover</b>: The Chicago Park District plans to repair and regild the Gold Lady starting in May. The $1 million job includes removing the 24-foot statue’s worn and flaking gold leaf and applying a new layer of the microthin substance over the entire work.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wtuhyil-l-yd/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>FROM THE PRESS BOX 🏀⚾🏈</h4><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="line-height: inherit; margin-top: 20px; margin-bottom: 20px; margin-left: 24px; padding: 0px; list-style-type: disc;" id="rte-f58f1182-2d00-11f1-be08-2f3a7c335d08"><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wtuhyil-l-yk/" target="_blank" ><b>Bulls waive Jaden Ivey</b></a>: Ivey has gone on a series of rants about religion. href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wtuhyil-l-ji/" target="_blank" >Young South Side team dominates competitive cheerleading</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wtuhyil-l-jd/" target="_blank" >Elleiana Green</a></h5><p>Naliyah Saintil, a 13-year-old from Greater Grand Crossing, dances and tumbles with the South Side cheer team Black Onyx Allstars, where her rapid rise — she recently was named a captain — has turned her father, John Barnes, from a longtime basketball coach into a "full-blown cheer dad ... I’ve got about 45 shirts to prove it," Barnes said, laughing.</p><p>Naliyah’s rise on the team reflects something bigger happening. Earlier this month, the South Side program won across all three of its team levels at Xtreme Spirit Kalahari National Competition in Wisconsin Dells. The wins secured each squad another bid to nationals in Orlando and cemented the Black Onyx Allstars’ reputation as one of the Midwest’s most dominant competitive cheer programs.</p><p>With 18 first-place finishes since the program began in 2024, and five of those awarded just this season, the team hopes to continue adding hardware at upcoming competitions in Grayslake, Illinois, and in Nashville, Tennessee.</p><p>Black Onyx, a nonprofit organization, was started by Imani Cobb and Endigo Allen, former elementary school cheer teammates at Betty Shabazz Academy in Greater Grand Crossing. In 2023, the school asked them to hold a cheer clinic for a few months, then put on an exhibition. But that turned into coaching a cheer team for a full season, and by 2024, the two had decided to go independent, starting their nonprofit to expand access to cheer in the Black community.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wtuhyil-l-jh/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>WATCH: MEET THE BLACK ONYX CHEER TEAM ▶️</h4><div class="Enhancement" </div></div><hr><p></p><h4>YOUR DAILY QUESTION ☕️</h4><p>Yesterday, we asked you: <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wtuhyil-l-tl/" target="_blank" >What do you think of freezing the minimum wage for tipped workers?</a></p><p>Here’s some of what you said, edited for length and clarity.</p><p>"Business owners need to pay their workers as little as possible in order to maximize their profits and stay competitive. That’s capitalism. I’m a worker, so I’m on the side of polices that raise wages for workers, necessarily at a cost to their bosses. This isn’t some complicated policy question ... It’s a class struggle. And right now, a majority of City Council [members have] declared themselves to be on the side of the bosses against workers." — <i>Sean Duffy</i></p><p>"Enforce the current law rather than [penalize] restaurants. Abolishing the minimum tipped wage will absolutely force owners to reduce hours and jobs, and ultimately move to automated ordering. So much for helping the workers." — <i>Judy Magee</i></p><p>"The debate over a tipped minimum wage is exactly the distraction that rich, powerful elites want. Instead of recognizing that the economic system is unsustainable for most working-class people — restaurant owners, servers, back of the house etc. — people are being divided ... Once enough people realize that our current economic model works best for those who are already financially well-off ... we might be able to agree upon a system that actually benefits society as a whole." — <i>Jacob Caplan</i> </p><p>"I know my bartender friend does not like this law because she's worried she'll make less tips. If I know wait staff is getting a higher amount of hourly money ... the tipping percentage needs to go down. And the restaurants that currently tack on 20% need to lower it. Then it would be like Europe, where waiters actually get paid, and people don't tip." — <i>Lisa Janoschka</i></p><hr><p></p><h4>PICTURE CHICAGO 📸</h4><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div entries.author: Matt Moore

entries.title: Bystander shot, vehicle hit by gunfire crashes on Near North Side
entries.summary: <p>Someone inside a white car opened fire at a red car, but instead hit a woman walking nearby as the attack also caused a crash on the Near North Side Monday night. </p><p>Around 9:30 p.m., someone inside a white sedan approached a red vehicle in the first block of East Chicago Avenue and began shooting, Chicago police said. </p><p>A 25-year-old woman was walking outside and was struck in the thigh by gunfire, police said. She was taken to Northwestern Memorial Hospital where she was in fair condition. </p><p>The red vehicle was struck by gunfire and crashed into a black SUV, video shows. </p><p>Area 3 detectives were investigating. </p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Mourners say final goodbyes to fallen firefighter Michael Altman: 'It’s clear he was well loved'
entries.summary: <p>Friends and loved ones of fallen Chicago firefighter Michael Altman said their final goodbyes during a funeral Tuesday morning in southwest suburban Oak Lawn.</p><p>The private funeral service was held at the Blake-Lamb funeral home at 4727 W. 103rd St. after planned services on Friday <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/03/26/funeral-for-chicago-firefighter-killed-in-rogers-park-blaze-canceled-after-wife-goes-into-labor" target="_blank" >were canceled when Altman’s wife</a> went into labor Thursday.</p><p>Despite steady rain, first responders and community members lined the street outside the funeral home as Altman’s casket, draped in a Chicago flag, was carried to a procession bound for his final resting place at Holy Sepulchre Cemetery in Alsip.</p><p>Altman, a firefighter and EMT, died March 17 from injuries he suffered the day before while <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/16/extra-alarm-blaze-erupts-at-a-rogers-park-building-possibly-injuring-firefighter" target="_blank" >fighting a fire</a> in a Rogers Park apartment. The blaze was in a four-story building in the 1700 block of West North Shore Avenue. Altman fell after the floor collapsed, fire officials said.</p><p>Robert Podvin, a former Cook County correctional officer, never met 32-year-old Altman but said he came to pay his respects to the family and to the first-responders who served alongside him.</p><p>Standing across the street from the funeral home, Podvin said the loss of a first responder is never easy, but he hopes the outpouring of support shows how deeply Altman was loved and offers some comfort to his family.</p><p>“It’s clear he was well loved,” said Podvin, 69. “[Altman] died a tragic death and it leaves you speechless. I hope he rests in peace.”</p><p>Altman had been with the department less than two years. He served on Truck 47 in Edgewater and came “from a long history of public service in the Chicago Fire Department,” Fire Commissioner Annette Nance-Holt said on the day Altman died.</p><p>Altman’s grandfather, Edward P. Altman, served as the fire commissioner from 1996 until 1999. Three of his sons — Edward F., James and Altman’s father, Michael — followed in his footsteps and were also firefighters. Altman’s father rose to the rank of battalion chief.</p><p>Mayor Brandon Johnson told reporters the city “not only lost a public servant, but we lost a hero.”</p><p>Former suburban firefighter Michel Gruber said it was important to show support for the family of a firefighter who died in the line of duty.</p><p>“When a family has suffered such a tragic loss as this, support is the biggest thing that can be shown,” said Gruber, 70. “He didn't die alone and will never be forgotten. His death was not in vain.”</p><p>Sheaves Slate, 27, is <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/23/chicago-crime-fire-michael-altman-cfd-sheaves-slate" target="_blank" >charged with</a> murder, residential arson and aggravated arson injuring a firefighter in connection with the fire. Prosecutors allege Slate, who had lived in the four-flat, was feeling “suicidal” about his “poor relationship with friends” and used a lighter to set fire to a mattress in inside a third-floor unit.</p><p>Slate was ordered detained at a hearing last week. As they had at an earlier hearing, firefighters again packed the courtroom in support of Altman.<br></p>
entries.author: Cindy Hernandez

entries.title: Junta de CPS aprueba contrato para la nueva directora ejecutiva Macquline King con sólo 1 voto en contra
entries.summary: <p>La Junta de Educación de Chicago aprobó un contrato para la nueva directora ejecutiva del distrito escolar, Macquline King, un talento local que ha liderado el distrito de manera interina durante los últimos 10 meses. <a class="Link" href="https://www.cps.edu/press-releases/2026/march/chicago-board-of-education-selects-dr.-macquline-king-as-superintendentceo-of-chicago-public-schools/" target="_blank" >King tendrá un contrato de tres años</a> con un salario inicial de $380,000, lo que representa $40,000 más que su predecesor, Pedro Martínez.</p><p>La votación representó una rara unión de la junta escolar, que es parcialmente electa y parcialmente nombrada. Sólo Jennifer Custer, quien representa al lejano lado noroeste, votó en contra del contrato de King.</p><p>Tras la votación casi unánime, King recibió una ovación de pie.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-3f0000" name="module-3f0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>King expresó que tiene confianza en que las Escuelas Públicas de Chicago (CPS) se están moviendo en la dirección correcta a pesar de “los serios desafíos” que enfrenta el distrito. Dijo que trabajará para construir sobre el progreso que los estudiantes han logrado graduarse a tiempo de la secundaria y obtener créditos universitarios a través de clases de inscripción dual.</p><p>Ahora, afirmó, la misión es “construir un puente de excelencia” a través de la experiencia de kínder a doceavo grado, asegurando que haya un currículo sólido para cada materia.</p><p>“Protegeremos el progreso que hemos logrado con todo lo que tenemos”, dijo King. “Nuestro camino a seguir es claro. Está grabado en nuestro plan estratégico centrado en una cosa: la experiencia del estudiante”.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Los miembros de la junta escolar de Chicago se tomaron una selfie con la nueva directora ejecutiva, Macquline King, después de votar para aprobar su contrato de tres años, poniendo fin a una búsqueda de meses por un líder permanente.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Ashlee Rezin/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“Lo más importante, escucharé a nuestros estudiantes”, añadió King. “Quiero que cada niño sienta un profundo sentido de pertenencia en nuestras escuelas. Como graduada orgullosa de CPS, me veo reflejada en los ojos de nuestros estudiantes”.</p><p>King no fue nombrada originalmente como finalista para el puesto permanente, pero los miembros de la junta <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2026/03/13/cps-board-names-3-finalists-to-lead-district-interim-ceo-king-former-nyc-east-baton-rouge-schools-chiefs" target="_blank" >la añadieron a la lista corta hace unas semanas</a>. El apoyo rápidamente se reunió en torno a King, especialmente después de que un ex canciller de las escuelas públicas de la ciudad de Nueva York se retirara de la contienda.</p><p>Custer expresó su decepción porque King fue añadida nuevamente en el último minuto.</p><p>“De repente tenemos mucha presión justo al final para volver a incluirla”, dijo Custer. “Este realmente no fue el proceso que la gente de Chicago buscaba, y este no fue el proceso que habíamos establecido”.</p><p>También mencionó que el personal escolar en su distrito le dijo que creían que la comunicación de King era deficiente, describiéndola como “no tan frecuente, no tan a menudo, algo de último minuto”.</p><p>Hasta ahora, Custer es el único miembro en funciones que ha anunciado su candidatura para presidenta de la junta escolar. Dijo que si gana, no buscará automáticamente reemplazar a King, añadiendo que quiere darle una oportunidad para demostrar su valor.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>La miembro de la junta escolar Jennifer Custer fue la única en votar “no” para nombrar a Macquline King como directora ejecutiva de las Escuelas Públicas de Chicago. Dijo que el personal escolar en su distrito del lejano lado noroeste había expresado que la comunicación de King era deficiente.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Ashlee Rezin/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>La junta concluye un 'largo y extraño viaje' para contratar a un director ejecutivo permanente</p><p>Michilla Blaise, quien fue nombrada por el alcalde Brandon Johnson, dijo que King cumple con todos sus requisitos: una mujer negra que vive en el lado oeste y tiene una amplia experiencia en CPS.</p><p>También dijo que cree que King trabajará con ella para averiguar cómo abordar las escuelas con pocas inscripciones, uno de los principales problemas que enfrenta el distrito escolar y las comunidades del lado oeste que Blaise representa. Blaise comentó que se necesitaría una “verdadera asociación” para formular una visión para esas escuelas y llevarla “hasta su realización sin simplemente cerrar puertas”.</p><p>Ellen Rosenfeld, quien representa al lado norte cercano, dijo que ha apoyado a King desde el principio, pero señaló que la <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/chicago-school-board/2026/02/13/is-cps-superintendent-search-on-track-or-stalled-depends-who-you-ask-on-school-board" target="_blank" >búsqueda de varios meses de un líder permanente</a> había sido “un largo y extraño viaje”.</p><p>Rosenfeld expresó que estaba impresionada con la valentía e independencia de King, probablemente en referencia a cuando King se enfrentó al alcalde y sus aliados en la junta escolar al negarse a presentar un presupuesto que podría haber empeorado la deuda del distrito.</p><p>“Continuaremos exigiendo transparencia, rendición de cuentas y participación significativa”, añadió Rosenfeld. “Felicidades, vamos a trabajar”.</p><p>Yesenia López, cuyo distrito del lado suroeste fue frecuentemente atacado durante la intensa actividad de aplicación de la ley de inmigración el otoño pasado, dijo que vio crecer el liderazgo de King durante ese tiempo.</p><p>“Ella estaba abierta a colaborar en la mejor respuesta posible en el momento en que operaba el Midway Blitz, afectando a muchas de nuestras comunidades inmigrantes”, expresó Lopez.</p><p>Pero le pidió a King que incluyera a líderes latinos y asiáticoestadounidensesen su gabinete para que sus asesores representaran la diversidad de los estudiantes del distrito y “comprendieran la importancia de nuestros estudiantes de inglés, de nuestras comunidades diversas, de la educación especial y del programa de Safe Passage”.</p><p>Muchos elogian la decisión de la junta escolar de contratar a 'una de los suyos'</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Dian Palmer, presidenta del SEIU Local 73, dijo que esperaba que la nueva directora ejecutiva, Macquline King, considerara su “experiencia vivida” como directora de CPS, madre y estudiante mientras lidera el distrito.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Ashlee Rezin/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>King también recibió apoyos de líderes sindicales, miembros de la comunidad y varios concejales de la ciudad.</p><p>Dian Palmer, presidenta del Sindicato Internacional de Empleados de Servicios (SEIU) Local 73, que representa a miles de trabajadores de CPS, dijo que sus miembros confiaban en que King sería el tipo de líder que puede “sentarse a la mesa con nosotros y tratar a nuestro personal de apoyo no como una partida en el presupuesto, sino como el corazón palpitante de este sistema”.</p><p>“Cuando hablamos de quién debería liderar esas escuelas, no sólo hablamos de un título”, afirmó. “Hablamos de una visión, hablamos de valores”.</p><p>Al igual que varios otros oradores, Palmer dijo que cree que la “experiencia vivida” de King en CPS le servirá bien.</p><p>King es graduada de CPS y sus dos hijos adultos asistieron a CPS. Impartió clases en Chicago antes de convertirse en directora de dos pequeñas escuelas primarias: una escuela mayormente negra en el lado sur y una escuela diversa en el lado norte.</p><p>Tiene experiencia liderando durante los cierres masivos de escuelas de CPS en 2013, dando la bienvenida a niños en una nueva escuela después de que la suya cerrara y liderando durante la pandemia de COVID-19.</p><p>“Ella sabe que el progreso sostenible en nuestras escuelas no puede lograrse a través de mandatos de arriba hacia abajo”, comentó Palmer. “Se construye ladrillo a ladrillo a través de acciones honestas, respeto mutuo y sacrificio compartido”.</p><p>Kia Banks, presidenta de la Asociación de Directores y Administradores de Chicago, dijo que King ha demostrado disposición para colaborar con los líderes escolares. El grupo se convirtió en un sindicato oficial de negociación colectiva para los directores de CPS en 2023 y finalizó su primer contrato en otoño bajo la dirección de King.</p><p>“Reconocemos que no siempre estaremos de acuerdo”, dijo Banks. “Habrá momentos de tensión. Habrá decisiones difíciles, y siempre habrá prioridades compitiendo”.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Jackson Potter, vicepresidente del Sindicato de Maestros de Chicago (CTU), dijo que Macquline King ha demostrado ser un tipo diferente de líder y aplaudió cómo ha manejado hasta ahora el cierre a mitad de año de dos escuelas secundarias chárter.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Ashlee Rezin/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Pero Banks afirmó que confía en que los sindicatos, la junta escolar y King pueden encontrar un terreno común para avanzar en el distrito escolar.</p><p>Jackson Potter, vicepresidente del Sindicato de Maestros de Chicago (CTU), expresó su esperanza de que el ascenso de King a directora ejecutiva represente un cambio comparado a cuando los líderes anteriores de CPS “se sometían a los megamillonarios y privatizadores”.</p><p>Potter dijo que King demostró que era diferente de los ex directores ejecutivos en cómo manejó a Aspira, una red de escuelas chárter que está a punto de cerrar esta semana tras quedarse sin fondos.</p><p>“Su trabajo para asegurar que los estudiantes de Aspira se gradúen con la menor interrupción posible, a pesar de las graves e irresponsables acciones del operador chárter, es digno de elogiarse”, afirmó.</p><p>Los líderes sindicales y los miembros del Concejo Municipal también mencionaron la gestión del presupuesto por parte de King el año pasado. Ella heredó un déficit de $734 millones cuando asumió como directora ejecutiva interina en junio, pero se negó a presentar un presupuesto que incluyera la posibilidad de préstamos, como deseaba la oficina del alcalde.</p><p>La concejal Stephanie Coleman (16°) felicitó a King en nombre del Caucus Negro de concejales de Chicago.</p><p>“No podríamos estar más orgullosos de los logros que ha alcanzado, especialmente en el último y más difícil presupuesto”, dijo Coleman. “La Dra. King ha hecho un trabajo heroico equilibrando el presupuesto, sin días de licencia, sin días de clases perdidos, y la felicito a usted y a su equipo”.</p><p>La concejal Jeanette Taylor (20°), que preside el Comité de Educación del Concejo, expresó su entusiasmo porque la junta eligió “a una de los suyos” para liderar el distrito.</p><p>“Sabemos que la junta ha tomado la decisión correcta, y estoy emocionada de que vayamos a regresar a la información y la inspiración”, afirmó Taylor.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure-credit"><p>Ashlee Rezin/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>La representante estatal Ann Williams, cuyo distrito incluye amplias zonas del lado norte, dijo que estaba “encantada” con el nombramiento de King y la aplaudió por hacerse disponible para los legisladores estatales a un nivel que “no hemos visto en varios años” de parte de un jefe de CPS.</p><p>“Esa asociación, esa colaboración, creo que será invaluable mientras luchamos por enfrentar los desafíos de financiamiento y algunos de los problemas a corto y largo plazo en los que todos necesitamos trabajar juntos”, dijo Williams.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" >La Voz Chicago</a></p>
entries.author: Emmanuel Camarillo

entries.title: IDOT clears suburban road builder to resume work after investigation, lengthy 'pause' on contracts
entries.summary: <p>Builders Paving LLC, a Hillside road contractor run by a daughter and son-in-law of felon Sebastian “Sam” Palumbo, is being cleared to once again work on Illinois Department of Transportation projects as part of a just-announced agreement that appears to end a dispute that had threatened the company’s financial health and raised questions about potential fraud to taxpayers.</p><p>Overseen by Gov. JB Pritzker, IDOT gave little detail about the agreement — including why it was reached and what IDOT found while <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/07/06/palumbo-builders-paving-hillside-chicago-road-construction-illinois-department-of-transportation" >investigating Builders </a>and its suspected ties to Palumbo.</p><p>While helping oversee a decades-old family road-building empire involving other Chicago area companies, Palumbo, a brother and their father pleaded guilty in 1999 to a scam shortchanging the benefit plans for their union workers.</p><p>Two of the family’s companies back then, Palumbo Brothers, Inc., and Monarch Asphalt Company, also admitted to overbilling taxpayers on numerous road projects.</p><p>Those two companies were permanently banned from state and federal projects, as were “all existing or later created affiliates and successors,” including a Hillside-based business called Orange Crush LLC, according to IDOT.</p><p>Palumbo also was barred from participating in state or federal road projects.</p><p>While Palumbo was running Orange Crush more recently, records filed with the Illinois secretary of state’s office showed his daughter Kaitlyn Palumbo Gandy was part of a firm called Five Sisters Management LLC that served as a manager of Orange Crush.</p><p>Gandy and her husband were, and remain, top executives with Builders Paving and a larger consortium called Builders Companies, which also includes Builders Asphalt.</p><p>The Chicago Sun-Times asked IDOT officials in 2024 whether they were aware of the father-daughter business connection — and that Builders and Orange Crush appeared to share a construction yard in the west suburbs and some administrative functions. The agency launched an investigation and put a “pause” on awarding new contracts to Builders.</p><p>The company, though, was allowed to continue bidding, and it ended up the low bidder on two dozen road projects totaling more than $50 million that weren’t allowed to begin amid the inquiry.</p><p>IDOT tried to determine if and how Palumbo was involved with Builders in violation of the debarments.</p><p>William Dwyer, a lawyer representing Palumbo and Builders, has insisted that Palumbo was not involved in the Builders operation, but he also refused to say whether Palumbo had ever served as an advisor, even if unpaid, for Builders or helped launch the enterprise years earlier.</p><p>Dwyer has also disputed IDOT’s claim that Orange Crush was subject to IDOT’s debarment.</p><p>“Facts and the truth have won out over your malicious campaign,” Dwyer told a reporter Tuesday.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" class="Figure-credit"><p>Instagram, Facebook, Builders Companies</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p> Gandy’s husband, Ryan Gandy, recently addressed the settlement by writing to employees:</p><p>“The long-fought battle to defend our integrity and preserve our ability to contract with IDOT is a humbling reminder of the fragile prequalification process when contracting with governmental agencies. Our victory validates our internal control procedures and strict adherence to job specific specifications.”</p><p>“We will now begin the process of reviewing subcontractor pricing, capacities to perform, material and labor pricing on <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2026/02/15/illinois-road-construction-season-approaching-palumbo-family-builders-paving-idot" target="_blank" >the 24 pending IDOT contracts</a>. In the near future we will provide a more detailed update.”</p><p>It’s unclear if and how the projects that have been delayed will rise in price.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2024/12/20/road-construction-builders-paving-asphalt-sam-palumbo-brothers-debarred-illinois" target="_blank" >Another generation of the Palumbo family becomes a larger player in regional road construction</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Portraying the long-running dispute as "distractions," Gandy's statement said IDOT determined that "based on its review of the companies' corporate records, IDOT's own records, and the Builders Companies' certifications, IDOT has concluded there is insufficient evidence to support the conclusion that the Builders Companies were not responsible bidders under the Illinois Procurement Code."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a JB Pritzker and Illinois Transportation Secretary Gia Biagi." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/065d912/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6926x3887+0+365/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F5c%2F95%2Faabda52b4f9c8254a06eec631063%2Fjb-gia.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/89b8ce6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6926x3887+0+365/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F5c%2F95%2Faabda52b4f9c8254a06eec631063%2Fjb-gia.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/065d912/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6926x3887+0+365/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F5c%2F95%2Faabda52b4f9c8254a06eec631063%2Fjb-gia.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Gov. JB Pritzker and Illinois Transportation Secretary Gia Biagi.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Sun-Times file photos</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Pritzker’s transportation secretary, Gia Biagi, wouldn’t comment.</p><p>Her office released a statement saying:</p><p>“Throughout this process, the department has prioritized the integrity of the procurement process and the best interests of taxpayers. Both sides in this matter have reached terms of an agreement.”</p><p>“They include dismissing the appeal, pending court order” — <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/06/06/palumbo-builders-paving-hillside-illinois-transportation-governor-pritzker-construction-roads" target="_blank" >Builders sued IDOT last year</a> to try to end the logjam — “as well as waiving any other claims, allowing contracts that have been put on pause to be awarded and providing guidance for future participation on projects under certain conditions.”</p><p>“We look forward to enacting a solution and getting to work on another historic construction season in Illinois.”</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/08/29/cook-county-board-toni-preckwinkle-illinois-department-of-transportation-builders-paving-palumbo" target="_blank" >Road builder tied to Palumbo family members wins more than $33 million in contracts from Cook County</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Robert Herguth

entries.title: Justin Fields' first year with Bears helped him land new gig with Chiefs
entries.summary: <p>PHOENIX — Justin Fields’ first year with the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> helped get him his latest job.</p><p>Speaking at the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/30/bears-ryan-poles-eyes-nfl-draft-picks-remake-defense-dennis-allen-nfl-annual-meetings-2026-phoenix" target="_blank" >NFL’s Annual Meeting</a> on Tuesday, coach Andy Reid said the team traded for Fields in part because of his experience in head coach Matt Nagy’s system. Fields played his rookie season in 2021 under Nagy before the Bears fired the head coach.</p><p>Fields will serve as insurance in case star quarterback Patrick Mahomes can’t recover from his torn ACL in time to start the season opener. Fields will act as the Chiefs’ starter throughout the offseason program.</p><p>“Not only do we like him as a player, but he was also in this offense as a rookie with Matt Nagy,” Reid said. So it’s not going to be completely foreign to him. “I look forward to getting him in here — for the most part because he’s a good player and if Pat can’t go for whatever reason, you’ve got a legitimate guy in there who can play and win games for you.”</p><p>The Bears traded Fields, their former first-round pick, to the Steelers two years ago. He went 4-2 as the starter before being replaced by Russell Wilson. The Jets signed Fields last offseason and made him the starter, only for him to go 2-7. The Chiefs traded for him two weeks ago, giving up a 2027 sixth-round pick.</p><p>Reid said Tuesday that Mahomes was progressing well in his rehab.</p><p>"He’s doing good," he said. "It’s hard to measure if he’s ahead of schedule or not."<br></p>
entries.author: Patrick Finley

entries.title: The Jaden Ivey fiasco sheds more light on a Bulls failing front office
entries.summary: <p>SAN ANTONIO — It needed a “Godfather I” ending.</p><p>Instead, it felt like the final episode of “The Sopranos” — incomplete and fade to black.</p><p>That was the disappointment to come out of Monday evening. <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bulls" >Bulls</a> ownership had an opening to take care of all family business at once but stalled out after <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bulls/2026/03/30/league-source-says-bulls-working-exit-strategy-for-jaden-ivey" >dismissing guard Jaden Ivey</a> for conduct detrimental to the team.</p><p>It should have played out like this: Ivey sent packing, followed by the front office that did no homework on him before acquiring the one-man distraction from Detroit. Then at the same time a deal worked out with Billy Donovan in which he can continue coaching if he wants, choosing the general manager/executive he wants in place, and once he feels like his coaching bucket is full, he takes the Brad Stevens express lane to a front office job to help with personnel decisions.</p><p>An ending still in play but now major long shot more than reality.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bulls/2026/03/30/league-source-says-bulls-working-exit-strategy-for-jaden-ivey" >Bulls waive guard Jaden Ivey after anti-Pride remarks</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>What is reality for the organization is that executive vice president of basketball operations Arturas Karnisovas continues proving that the job is too big for him. The Ivey acquisition itself is not grounds for Karnisovas being let go. What it was, however, was the latest flammable excrement dropped on a dumpster pile that was already in flames.</p><p>Before Ivey even stepped foot on Chicago soil there were rumblings from Detroit that the shooting guard was a “preacher.” They undersold it. His interviews became sermons in which he was asking reporters if they’ve been “saved” and if they “fornicated before marriage.”</p><p>My bad, brah, I was just checking in on how your knee felt.</p><p>It’s great that he has religion, but the relationship between reporter and player isn’t built like that after just few interactions. While reporters were left feeling like they didn’t need to be preached to from a 20-something kid a few years removed from walking around with a silver spoon in his mouth on the AAU circuit, his Bulls teammates were left shaking their heads at just the latest distraction provided by their front office.</p><p>Not one Bulls player was shedding a tear for Ivey’s removal from the roster.</p><p>And not one Bulls player — former or current — has believed in the Karnisovas plan since the start of the season.</p><p>It is over.</p><p>The trades have been a disaster, the draft picks a joke, and the idea of chasing “competitive integrity” over increasing draft odds a complete misguided exercise in organizational malpractice.</p><p>The Ivey fiasco shouldn’t be the straw that broke the camel’s back as much as a reminder that the camel had damaged legs, one working eye, and a bizarre-looking hump for years.</p><p>If Karnisovas stays, there’s a growing chance that Donovan walks at the end of the season. Then the Reinsdorfs will leave Karnisovas with a coaching search, a lottery draft choice, and free agent decisions with money to spend.</p><p>This front office can’t even get trade dumps at the deadline right, let alone being trusted to map out the immediate future of an organization that has gone from bad to national embarrassment.</p><p>In all fairness to team president Michael Reinsdorf, not one NBA person thought Karnisovas was a bad hire six-plus years ago. In fact, the team was widely applauded for the decision. Billion-dollar companies need to pivot when decisions go bad, however, and that’s where the Bulls are at.</p><p>Ivey’s social media rants showed the make-up of the man and why he didn’t fit with the Bulls locker room. More importantly, they put the spotlight directly on the failed front office that brought him to Chicago in the first place.</p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module class="PagePromo-description">Despite what executive vice president Arturas Karnisovas says and believes, the NBA is about collecting talent and getting that talent as high as possible in the draft. That’s why the Bulls’ only focus for the rest of the season should be locking down the best draft odds they can.</div> <div class="PagePromo-byline"><div class="PagePromo-date"> <bsp-timestamp data-timestamp=1774811184000 data-promo="true"> <template data-date-tpl>[month] [day], [year], [hour]:[minute][ampm] [timezone]</template> <span data-date> </span> </bsp-timestamp><span class="dot"></span></div> <div class="PagePromo-author"> <span>By </span><strong><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/authors/joe-cowley" >Joe Cowley </a></strong> </div> <div class="PagePromo-readingTime"> <span class="dot-divider"></span> <svg class="PagePromo-readingTime-icon"> <use xlink:href="#icon-clock-reading"></use> </svg> <span class="PagePromo-readingTime-text"> <bsp-time-to-read data-article-word-count="603"></bsp-time-to-read> read </span> </div> </div> </div> </div> </div> </div> <div class="PageList-footer"> </div> </bsp-list-loadmore><p></p>
entries.author: Joe Cowley

entries.title: Crystal Lake man charged with sexually assaulting woman in Loop parking garage
entries.summary: <p>A Crystal Lake man has been accused of sexually assaulting a woman in a Loop parking garage Monday.</p><p>Garland Benton, 45, faces charges of aggravated battery in a public place, aggravated criminal sexual abuse and attempted aggravated criminal sexual assault, Chicago police said. </p><p>Benton is scheduled to appear for a detention hearing Tuesday at the Leighton Criminal Courthouse. </p><p>A 20-year-old woman was walking in the first block of North Michigan Avenue at 6:35 a.m. when, police said, Benton allegedly approached and assaulted her. Bystanders intervened, and Benton fled before being arrested by police shortly after.</p><p>The woman was taken to Northwestern Memorial Hospital, police said.<br></p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Niños de kínder aprenden a andar en bicicleta con nuevas ruedas en primaria de Gage Park
entries.summary: <p>Los estudiantes de la Escuela Primaria Sidney Sawyer en el vecindario de Gage Park en Chicago recibieron una flota de 24 bicicletas el lunes.</p><p>Para algunos de los estudiantes, era su primera vez montando una bicicleta.</p><p>“Estaba un poco nerviosa porque era mi primera vez”, dijo la estudiante de kinder, Monsé Maldonado. “Pensé que me iba a caer, pero no. Salió bien”.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-aa0000" name="module-aa0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>Abogados de la firma Motorcycle Safety Lawyers ayudaron a recaudar el dinero para la organización sin fines de lucro All Kids Bike, que donó el equipo. La firma de abogados dice que trabajan con escuelas desfavorecidas que tienen el espacio para enseñar un programa de educación sobre bicicletas. La escuela también recibirá un currículo para enseñar a los niños a montar.</p><p>Los niños aprenderán primero utilizando el método de bicicleta de equilibrio. Se centrarán en el equilibrio y la dirección primero sin usar los pedales.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a
