entries.author: Candace Dane Chambers

entries.title: Ryan Poles eyes long-term future with QB Caleb Williams but Bears need a 'green light' first
entries.summary: <p>PHOENIX — General manager <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/30/bears-ryan-poles-eyes-nfl-draft-picks-remake-defense-dennis-allen-nfl-annual-meetings-2026-phoenix" target="_blank" >Ryan Poles</a> thinks of it as a crossroads — only better.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> quarterback Caleb Williams’ rookie year was a red light — inconsistent and not good enough. Last year’s season of breathtaking finishes was a yellow light, allowing the Bears general manager and millions of fans to dream about the quarterback’s future.</p><p>This year, Poles hopes, will be a green light — a bright beacon confirming Williams’ status as the Bears’ long-promised, long-term quarterback. Poles has allowed himself to tap the accelerator, emotionally, inching forward as he waits for the traffic light to turn green.</p><p>“It’s good to <i>almost</i> feel like you’ve got it,” he said. “If you go with a traffic light, it went red as a rookie to yellow. You can creep forward a little bit and start having some of these conversations to anticipate it. But we need him to give us a green light.</p><p>“I think we’ll find out this year about his progress.”</p><p>Williams’ on-field development is up to the quarterback and coach Ben Johnson, Poles said.</p><p>“Consistency,” he said, “is what I’m looking for.”</p><p>If Williams grows the way the Bears believe he will, Poles will be left with the ultimate good-problem-to-have: finding a way to make the former No. 1 overall pick one of the richest players in the sport — as early as January — while building a team around him that’s capable of winning a championship.</p><p>In a sit-down interview with the Sun-Times at the NFL's Annual Meeting this week, Poles acknowledged all that awaits Williams — and the Bears — in his critical third year. The GM is on the verge of finding out whether the team has solved one of the most vexing problems in franchise history.</p><p>“Not just Bears history — football history,” Poles said. “There’s been some unbelievable teams that can’t get the quarterback right, and it doesn’t lead to much. Then there are times where the quarterback’s right — it usually bears the fruit that you’re looking for and results in being a contender and a championship-caliber team.</p><p>“We’re making progress. That is exciting, to even be thinking about planning for that. It’s exciting. But then there’s a challenge after that, too.”</p><p>Williams signed a four-year, $39.5 million contract with a fifth-year team option on the eve of training camp in 2024, a standard deal for the No. 1 overall pick.</p><p>NFL rules dictate that the Bears can sign Williams to a contract extension as soon as next offseason. That’s exactly what the Bengals’ Joe Burrow and the Jaguars’ Trevor Lawrence, quarterbacks who were the first overall picks in 2020 and 2021, did. After only three seasons, Burrow signed a five-year, $275 million deal with $219 million guaranteed. Lawrence signed a five-year, $275 million deal after only three seasons, too, and got $200 million guaranteed.</p><p>Reaching a deal with Williams will be more complicated that simply adjusting the salaries to meet salary cap inflation, though. Williams doesn’t have an agent. Few athletes on the planet have negotiated a deal that large without one.</p><p>“I’m sure it’ll present challenges,” Poles said. “We haven’t gotten that far. But if and when we get to that point, we’ll embrace that challenge.”</p><p>The Bears are just beginning the research what a potential Williams contract extension could look like. Matt Feinstein, the team’s salary cap guru and vice president of football administration, will be in charge of presenting Poles with potential extension ideas months before the Bears are allowed to negotiate with their quarterback.</p><p>“It’s really a summer project,” Poles said. “Matt Feinstein is digging into it now. We’ve had some preliminary [internal] conversations because we want to take some of these things into consideration as we build the team.”</p><p>Poles knows his defense needs to get younger and cheaper if he's going to give right tackle Darnell Wright, another former first-round pick, a contract extension this offseason and Williams, perhaps, a new deal next year.</p><p>“Our offense is going to slowly get really expensive,” Poles said. “You want to continue to have young talent to come in and help us out on defense.”</p><p>Building a team around a pricey quarterback is a challenge that has flummoxed other general managers. The seven highest-paid quarterbacks in terms of guaranteed money at signing are the Browns’ Deshaun Watson, the Bills’ Josh Allen, Burrow, Lawrence, the Ravens’ Lamar Jackson, the Chargers’ Justin Herbert and the Cowboys’ Dak Prescott. Those seven have won just three playoff games in 10 chances since signing their extensions.</p><p>The Chiefs’ Patrick Mahomes, as always, remains the exception. Since signing a 10-year contract extension in 2020 — the $450 million total value remains the league’s largest —he’s won two Super Bowls and has gone 13-3 in the postseason.</p><p>Poles worked for the Chiefs when they drafted Mahomes, and when they extended his contract. It’s unfair to compare Williams to him, Poles said, citing everything from Mahomes having veteran passer Alex Smith as a mentor to Mahomes inheriting a better team.</p><p>Every once in a while, though, he sees some similarities between the two when projecting Williams' future.</p><p>“Obviously there were some moments that gave me that vibe,” he said. “Really, I need to make this an independent study. But I like the direction it’s going.”</p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-show-loadmore="true" entries.author: Patrick Finley

entries.title: Judge orders Trump administration to halt White House ballroom construction unless Congress OKs it
entries.summary: <p>WASHINGTON — A federal judge on Tuesday ordered the Trump administration to suspend its construction of a $400 million ballroom where it demolished the East Wing of the White House, barring construction work from proceeding without congressional approval.</p><p>U.S. District Judge Richard Leon in Washington granted a preservationist group’s request for a preliminary injunction that temporarily halts President Donald Trump’s ballroom project.</p><p>Leon, nominated to the bench by Republican President George W. Bush, concluded the National Trust for Historic Preservation is likely to succeed on the merits of its claims because “no statute comes close to giving the President the authority he claims to have.”</p><p>“The President of the United States is the steward of the White House for future generations of First Families. He is not, however, the owner!” the judge wrote.</p><p>Leon suspended enforcement of his order for 14 days, acknowledging that the case “raises novel and weighty issues, that halting an ongoing construction project “may raise logistical issues.” He also recognized that the administration is likely to appeal his decision.</p><p>The judge ruled that any construction work that’s necessary to ensure the safety and security of the White House is exempt from the scope of the injunction. Leon said he reviewed material that the government privately submitted to him before concluding that halting construction wouldn’t jeopardize national security.</p><p>Trump, in a social media post, criticized the trust for suing him over a project that he said is being built at no cost to taxpayers. “Doesn’t make much sense, does it?” he wrote.</p><p>The White House did not immediately respond to a request for comment on the ruling.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-a00000" name="image-a00000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" 2x" width="490" height="275" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A model of the White House and the new ballroom (right), on a table in the Oval Office of the White House.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Alex Brandon/Associated Press</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The preservationists sued to obtain an order pausing the ballroom project until it undergoes multiple independent reviews and receives congressional approval.</p><p>The White House announced the ballroom project over the summer. By late October, Trump had demolished the East Wing to make way for a ballroom that he said would fit 999 people. The White House said private donations, including from Trump himself, would pay for the planned construction of a 90,000-square-foot (8,400-square-meter) ballroom.</p><p>Trump proceeded with the project before seeking input from a pair of federal review panels, the National Capital Planning Commission and the Commission of Fine Arts. Trump has stocked both commissions with allies.</p><p>The trust sued in December. On Feb. 26, Leon rejected the preservationist group’s initial bid to temporarily halt the ballroom’s construction. He said the privately funded group had based its challenge on a “ragtag group” of legal theories and would have a better chance of success if it amended the lawsuit, which it did.</p><p>Leon said the ballroom plans are in the final stages of the design approval process. The administration has said above-ground construction on the ballroom would begin in April.</p><p>“We are two weeks away,” plaintiffs’ attorney Thaddeus Heuer said during a March 17 hearing. “The imminence is now imminent.”</p><p>During the hearing, Leon sounded skeptical of what he referred to as the government’s “shifting theories and shifting dynamics” for its arguments in the case.</p><p>“I don’t think it’s a new theory,” Justice Department attorney Jacob Roth told the judge.</p><p>Leon expressed frustration at Roth’s attempts to equate the massive ballroom project with relatively modest construction work at the White House under previous administrations.</p><p>“This is an iconic symbol of this nation,” the judge said.</p><p>The administration argued that other presidents didn’t need congressional approval for previous White House renovation projects, large and small.</p><p>“Many of those projects were highly controversial in their time yet have since become accepted — even beloved — parts of the White House,” government attorneys wrote.</p><p>Leon rejected the administration’s claim that Congress gave the president virtually unilateral authority to construct anything on federal land in Washington, regardless of the funding source.</p><p>“This clearly is not how Congress and former Presidents have managed the White House for centuries, and this Court will not be the first to hold that Congress has ceded its powers in such a significant fashion!” the judge wrote.</p><p><i>Contributing: Associated Press writer Darlene Superville.</i></p><div entries.title: March Madness gets fans, alumni packing the room at Chicago's sports bars
entries.summary: <p>Sports pubs were slammed over the weekend with hoops fans swept up in <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports" >March Madness</a> — a welcomed business bump amid challenging times for the hospitality industry.</p><p>Venues with affiliations and partnerships with Big Ten schools fared even better, leveraging a deep roster of local alumni and other Midwest transplants. It also helped that the 18-team megaconference, headquartered in Rosemont, had four schools advance to the Elite Eight and two to the Final Four.</p><p>University of Illinois fans on Saturday morning were holding tables at Finley Dunne's Tavern right after the doors opened at 11 a.m. The Lake View bar, established by University of Illinois alumni, was packed shoulder-to-shoulder three hours before tipoff.</p><p>Finley Dunne's Tavern General Manager Peter Gertos estimates game days generate four to five times the revenue compared to a typical day.</p><p>“A lot of Big Ten grads live in the city now. It's insane how much that affects our sales,” Gertos said.</p><p>He said a partnership with the popular Illini-focused podcast, The Boardroom, has also boosted the bar's visibility and foot traffic. The podcast has a reserved table on game days and promotes the bar and related events to its audience and on social media.</p><p>Alumni ties and a partnership with the Purdue Club of Chicago, one of the oldest and largest alumni clubs at Purdue University, helped draw more than 100 customers to Park and Field in Logan Square on Saturday night. Most were aged between their 30s and 50s, reflecting a changing demographic as younger adults eschew alcohol.</p><p>“This was a nice bump with Purdue going to the Elite Eight,” Park and Field owner Dan Nalezny said.</p><p>A year ago, bad weather chilled business for most of April.</p><p>“We just try to get to May every year,” Nalezny said.</p><p>The Iowa Hawkeyes' magical run, culminating in their first trip to the Elite Eight since 1987, generated strong sales and buzz at Theory, a popular Iowa hangout in River North.</p><p>“Most of the local fan base hasn't been used to supporting the Hawkeyes this long into the tournament so it's a beautiful thing,” owner Joel Sorinsky, an Iowa alumni, said.</p><p>The upscale sports lounge also caters to Formula One fans, a rare target market in the city’s bar scene.</p><p>“We're just as busy for the Grand Prix tonight as we are for basketball,” Sorinsky told the Sun-Times Saturday. “You have to be a lot more strategic these days.”</p><h3>Game day buzz</h3><p>The Last Call Tavern Group has partnerships with colleges and alumni groups at each of its nine sports bars in the city. Duffy’s Tavern & Grille in Lincoln Park, for example, is a Michigan hangout while The Reveler in Roscoe Village caters to Arizona fans.</p><p>“I think we had nine teams that made the tournament this year, which is just phenomenal,” Last Call Tavern Group Chief Operating Officer Steve Kovaka said.</p><p>Reservations at Duffy’s were booked up well before Michigan’s dominating victory over Tennessee on Sunday afternoon.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-6a0000" name="image-6a0000"></a> alt="Michigan fans watch their team play in the Elite Eight at Duffy’s Tavern & Grille in Lincoln Park." 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e02a647/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+853/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa9%2F91%2F2f0951e04c518a4a30ddbcf2d3f9%2Fsportsbars-260401-009.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a05307f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+853/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa9%2F91%2F2f0951e04c518a4a30ddbcf2d3f9%2Fsportsbars-260401-009.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Michigan fans watch their team play in the Elite Eight at Duffy’s Tavern & Grille in Lincoln Park.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>On game days, the ownership group's bars are decorated in school colors and offer food specials, T-shirt giveaways, raffles and other prizes.</p><p>The company is adapting to the times too, as alcohol consumption hits record lows. They offer more nonalcoholic beers and mocktails, especially during Dry January, as well as <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/food-drink/2025/09/12/revolution-brewing-reverb-thc-cbd-drinks-slowing-beer-sales" >hemp-derived THC drinks</a>, a growing category for breweries, restaurants and liquor stores that’s drawn the ire of some local politicians and a potential federal ban.</p><p>“We're trying to stay as fluid as we can to understand and read the market,” Kovaka said. “It definitely has changed post-COVID, as far as drinking habits and patterns. We lost a whole generation that never had the exposure of turning 21 and going out to a sports bar or going out for a drink because we were closed.”</p><p>Big Onion Hospitality, which operates five sports bars in Chicago, reported a 7% year-over-year revenue increase from the tournament’s first two rounds, Sweet 16 and Elite Eight games, Marketing Vice President Tim Toomey said.</p><p>The company’s Fatpour Tap Works in Wicker Park draws Michigan Wolverines fans, Hopsmith Tavern in Gold Coast is a Michigan State hub and Woodie's Flat in Old Town primarily caters to Ohio State patrons.</p><p>“Each of those affiliations have been the biggest drivers, and I think most team-affiliated bars would say the same,” Toomey said. “The better the Big Ten does, the better we do.”</p><p>The locations offer a variety of entertainment and giveaways on game days, including slap koozies for early arrivals, carbon dioxide-powered cannons used for big plays and bands playing school fight songs.</p><p>Fatpour's introduction of the Munch Madness Platter, which includes mini hoop dipper cups, has also boosted sales.</p><p>Business appears to be improving this year at several Chicago sports bars, which were buoyed by the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/01/07/nfl-ticket-bears-packers-matchup-wild-card-game-pay-up" >Bears playoff run</a> in January, when sales typically freeze over.</p><p>The March rush, aided by St. Patrick’s Day celebrations, prompted Finley Dunne's to add a cook since the pub attracted new Illini customers amid their tournament run.</p><p>“This year has probably been our best year since I started five or six years ago,” Gertos said.</p>
entries.author: Chris Casacchia

entries.title: Police report says Tiger Woods had hydrocodone pills in his pocket at crash scene
entries.summary: <p>ORLANDO, Fla. — Tiger Woods' eyes were bloodshot and glassy, his pupils dilated and he had hydrocodone pills in his pocket when interviewed at the scene of his car crash last week in Florida, according to a sheriff's office report released Tuesday.</p><p>Woods' movements were slow and lethargic, he was sweating as he talked to deputies and told them he had taken prescription medication earlier in the morning, according to the incident report released by the Martin County Sheriff's Office. Woods told deputies he had been looking at his phone and fiddling with the radio before he clipped a truck in front of him, the report said.</p><p>Deputies found two white pills, which were identified as the opioid hydrocodone used to treat pain, in his pocket, the report said.</p><p>When asked by a deputy if he took any prescription medications, Woods said, "I take a few."</p><p>The golfer was traveling at high speeds on a beachside, residential road on Jupiter Island when his Land Rover clipped the truck and rolled onto its side, according to the sheriff's office, which noted Woods showed signs of impairment.</p><p>The truck had $5,000 in damage, according to the sheriff's report.</p><p>The truck driver and another person helped Woods out of his vehicle, with the golfer needing to climb out from the passenger side. Neither Woods nor the truck driver were injured.</p><p>During a field sobriety test, deputies noticed Woods limping and that he had a compression sock over his right knee. The golfer explained he had undergone seven back surgeries and over 20 leg operations and that his ankle seizes up while walking. Woods, who was hiccupping during the questioning, continuously moved his head during one of the sobriety tests and deputies had to instruct him several times to keep his head straight, the report said.</p><p>"Based on my observations of Woods, how he performed the exercises and based on my training, knowledge, and experience, I believed that Woods normal faculties were impaired, and he was unable to safely operate the motor vehicle," the deputy wrote after the tests.</p><p>Woods, 50, is the most influential figure in golf and has become as recognizable as any athlete in the world. The first person of Black heritage to win the Masters in 1997, he has captivated golf fans with records likely never to be broken.</p><p>But his injuries kept him from accomplishing more, including those suffered in a 2021 car crash that damaged his right leg so badly he said doctors considered amputation.</p><p>At this latest crash, Woods agreed to a Breathalyzer test that showed no signs of alcohol, but he refused a urine test, authorities said. He was arrested and released on bail eight hours later.</p><p>Woods' agent at Excel Sports, Mark Steinberg, has not responded to multiple messages seeking comment. No one from Woods' camp or the PGA Tour — he is on the board and is chairman of the committee reshaping the competition model — have commented since his arrest.</p><p>Woods, who has been involved in many crashes over the years, is charged with driving under the influence, property damage and refusal to submit to a lawful test. He is scheduled for arraignment April 23. Online court records do not list an attorney for him.</p><p>Under a change to Florida law last year, refusing a law enforcement officer's request to take a breath, blood or urine test became a misdemeanor, even for a first offense.</p>
entries.author: AP

entries.title: Judge blocks Trump order to end funding for National Public Radio, Public Broadcasting Service
entries.summary: <p>WASHINGTON — A federal judge on Tuesday agreed to permanently block the Trump administration from implementing a presidential directive to end federal funding for National Public Radio and the Public Broadcasting Service.</p><p>U.S. District Judge Randolph Moss in Washington ruled that President Donald Trump’s executive order to cease funding for NPR and PBS is unlawful and unenforceable. The judge said the First Amendment right to free speech “does not tolerate viewpoint discrimination and retaliation of this type.”</p><p>“It is difficult to conceive of clearer evidence that a government action is targeted at viewpoints that the President does not like and seeks to squelch,” wrote Moss, who was nominated to the bench by President Barack Obama, a Democrat. The judge noted that Trump’s executive order simply directs that all federal agencies “cut off any and all funding” to NPR, which is based in Washington, and PBS, based in Arlington, Virginia.</p><p>“The Federal Defendants fail to cite a single case in which a court has ever upheld a statute or executive action that bars a particular person or entity from participating in any federally funded activity based on that person or entity’s past speech,” the judge wrote.</p><p>Last year, Trump, a Republican, said at a news conference he would “love to” defund NPR and PBS because he believes they’re biased in favor of Democrats.</p><p>NPR accused the Corporation for Public Broadcasting of violating its First Amendment free speech rights when it moved to cut off its access to grant money appropriated by Congress. NPR also claims Trump wants to punish it for the content of its journalism.</p><p>Last August, CPB announced it would take steps toward closing itself down after being defunded by Congress.</p><p>Plaintiffs’ attorney Theodore Boutrous said Tuesday’s ruling is “a victory for the First Amendment and for freedom of the press.”</p><p>“As the Court expressly recognized, the First Amendment draws a line, which the government may not cross, at efforts to use government power — including the power of the purse — ‘to punish or suppress disfavored expression’ by others,” Boutrous said in a statement. “The Executive Order crossed that line.”<br></p>
entries.author: Michael Kunzelman | AP

entries.title: Ofrecen préstamos a bajo interés para los afectados por las inundaciones del año pasado
entries.summary: <p>Los residentes y empresas de seis condados, incluyendo Cook, que se vieron afectados por tormentas severas e inundaciones el año pasado pueden solicitar préstamos federales a bajo interés para gastos no cubiertos por el seguro, anunció la Administración de Pequeñas Empresas de Estados Unidos (SBA) a principios de este mes.</p><p>Los préstamos están disponibles tras la declaración de desastre de la SBA, la cual se emitió después de una solicitud del gobernador JB Pritzker el 12 de marzo, según un comunicado de la SBA.</p><p>Los préstamos son para daños relacionados con las tormentas que ocurrieron del 16 al 19 de agosto de 2025, un mes en el que algunos residentes de Chicago vieron sus <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/weather/2025/08/19/widespread-torrential-rainfall-leaves-several-inches-after-thunderstorms-sweep-through-chicago" target="_blank" >hogares inundados dos veces</a>. Los residentes de los condados de Cook, DuPage, Kane, Lake, McHenry y Will son elegibles para aplicar.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-a10000" name="module-a10000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>Los dos tipos de préstamos de la SBA disponibles son los Préstamos por Daños Físicos para propietarios de viviendas, inquilinos, negocios y organizaciones sin fines de lucro, y los Préstamos por Lesiones Económicas por Desastre para negocios y organizaciones sin fines de lucro. Los términos de los préstamos son un máximo de 30 años. Las empresas con crédito disponible en otros lugares pueden recibir un plazo máximo de siete años.</p><p>Los propietarios pueden solicitar hasta $500,000 para reparar o reemplazar sus hogares. Tanto los propietarios como los inquilinos pueden solicitar hasta $100,000 para daños a bienes personales como muebles, electrodomésticos y automóviles.</p><p>Las tasas de interés son tan bajas como el 3% para propietarios e inquilinos, alrededor del 3.6% para organizaciones sin fines de lucro y el 4% para empresas. Los pagos no son exigibles y los intereses no se acumulan hasta 12 meses después de la fecha de desembolso del primer préstamo, según la SBA. Los montos y términos del préstamo se establecen según la situación financiera de cada solicitante.</p><p>Las empresas y algunas organizaciones sin fines de lucro son elegibles para solicitar hasta $2 millones por daños físicos a activos como maquinaria, equipos e inventario. Incluso si una empresa u organización sin fines de lucro no sufrió daños físicos, todavía puede solicitar préstamos para cubrir deudas fijas, costos de nómina y otras facturas no pagadas debido al desastre.</p><p>La SBA ha abierto Centros de Alcance para Préstamos por Desastre en Chicago, en el Condado de Cook suburbano y en línea para ayudar a los residentes a recibir asistencia al solicitar los préstamos.</p><p>Los prestatarios tienen hasta el 15 de mayo para presentar solicitudes por daños a propiedades físicas y hasta el 16 de diciembre para presentar solicitudes por daños económicos. Los solicitantes deben tener números de seguro social para ser elegibles.</p><p>Para solicitar los préstamos, los solicitantes pueden visitar el sitio web de la SBA en <a class="Link" href="https://www.sba.gov/funding-programs/disaster-assistance" target="_blank" >sba.gov/disaster</a>.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Lizzie Kane

entries.title: Brookfield Zoo debuts litter of 10 African painted dog puppies
entries.summary: <p>A litter of 10 African painted dog pups now call Brookfield Zoo home.</p><p>The litter of five females and five males were born Nov. 25 to first-time mom Roanne, who was also born at the west suburban zoo six years ago.</p><p>Racquel Ardisana-Penney is associate director of carnivore and small mammal care and conservation at the zoo. She helped the new puppies reach their desired milestones like nutrition and vaccinations before incorporating them with their extended family.</p><p>“We're excited for our guests to finally see them,” Ardisana-Penney said. “It's very exciting for us to get to see them explore the outside world. They are going to be seeing a whole bunch of new things and getting a whole bunch of new experiences.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item"> <bsp-carousel </div> </div> </div></div><div class="FlickityCustomNavigation-wrapper"> <div class="FlickityCustomNavigation below"> <button class="FlickityCustomNavigation-button-prev" type="button" aria-label="Previous"> <svg class="FlickityCustomNavigation-button-icon"><use xlink:href="#icon-left"></use></svg> </button> <button class="FlickityCustomNavigation-button-next" type="button" aria-label="Next"> <svg class="FlickityCustomNavigation-button-icon"><use xlink:href="#icon-right"></use></svg> </button> </div> </div></div> </bsp-carousel> </div> </div><p>The new African painted dog pups, including mom Roanne and dad Kellan, tentatively explored their new home inside the Habitat Africa! The Savannah outdoor habitat for the first time on Tuesday.</p><p>Though initially timid, the pups followed their parents' lead into their habitat, with some pups showing more boldness and practicing their high-pitched, bird-like vocalizations known as twittering.</p><p>“Painted dogs make a lot of different vocalizations, but they're just communicating with each other their excitement," Ardisana-Penney said. “They also do a greeting ceremony, typically when they get up for the first time during the day, or when they get up for a hunt. It kind of gets them all excited and ready to go do something."</p><p>Although too young to hunt, Ardisana-Penney said the pups already are engaging in essential pack behavior.</p><p>Ardisana-Penney described African painted dogs as "highly social and strikingly patterned carnivores." Each has a unique coat, with patches of black, brown, white and yellow. In the wild, they typically hunt in packs to catch prey, such as antelopes, rodents and birds.</p><p>Kellan, the dad, was introduced to the zoo a year ago specifically to mate with the pups' mother.</p><p>“He was a good genetic match with Roanne, and so we brought him here for the purpose of breeding, " Ardisana-Penney said.</p><p>Ardisana-Penney said painted dogs can have large litters, perhaps 18 to 20, but seven or eight is more common for first-time mothers.</p><p>“We were surprised when we had 10,” she said.</p><p>According to zoo experts, African painted dogs once numbered in the hundreds of thousands across Africa. However, they require extensive ranges and decent pack sizes, and their population numbers have decreased in recent years. Fewer than 7,000 are estimated to remain in the wild.</p><p>That has made the small carnivores one of Africa’s most endangered predators.</p><p>Brookfield Zoo's last litter of African painted dog puppies was born in 2020. With the new additions, the zoo now has a total of 13 African painted dogs.</p><p>African painted dogs are part of the Species Survival Plan at Brookfield Zoo Chicago.</p><p>The Zoo also participates in Saving Animals From Extinction, a program of the Association of Zoos and Aquariums intended to identify survival threats and support recovery of endangered animals.</p><p></p>
entries.author: Araceli Gómez-Aldana | WBEZ

entries.title: 600 vacant city lots to hit the market Wednesday, including 55 for affordable housing
entries.summary: <p>More than 600 empty Chicago lots across 25 wards will hit the market for private purchase, the Department of Planning and Development announced Tuesday.</p><p>The $34 million in lots includes 30 parcels in West Englewood and South Chicago for <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/real-estate/2026/01/06/north-lawndale-housing-development-chicago-missing-middle" >Missing Middle housing</a> — a program started in 2024 aimed at turning vacant city land into housing for working middle-class families — and a 2.6-acre site in Austin that could support up to 140 new homes, according to the city agency.</p><p>They’ll go up for sale Wednesday on the city’s land sale portal <a class="Link" href="http://chiblockbuilder.com" target="_blank" ><u>ChiBlockBuilder.com</u></a>, alongside about 7,000 other available properties.</p><p>“These parcels offer unique opportunities for buyers to take ownership of their neighborhoods and contribute to local development goals,” said Department of Planning and Development Commissioner Ciere Boatright.</p><p>Approximately 500 lots will be put up at market rates for new projects: 55 for affordable housing, through the Chicago Department of Housing’s City Lots for Working Families program, and 66 for side yards, open space or urban agriculture, the agency said.</p><p>Mayor Brandon Johnson touted what the agency said was the largest spring inventory since the online portal launched in 2022 as a path to create more affordable housing.</p><p>"By making underutilized and vacant land available for residential construction and other uses, we are creating pathways to make housing more affordable while increasing housing availability citywide,” Johnson said. </p><p>The Missing Middle lots are set to become “owner-occupied residential buildings” with up to six units each along 69th Street in West Englewood and along South Baker and Buffalo Avenues in South Chicago, the agency said. The lots, which will be geared toward "market rate housing developers," come with up to $150,000 in construction assistance per unit and each parcel will be sold for $1.</p><p>The Austin site, which the city called “one of the largest contiguous development sites on the far West Side,” includes 47 lots earmarked for development as townhomes or multifamily buildings.</p><p>Applications for the lots are due in either 45 or 90 days.</p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: NFL commissioner Roger Goodell: Bears need to find stadium solution soon
entries.summary: <p>PHOENIX — Whether the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> decide to build a stadium in Arlington Heights or Hammond, Indiana, Roger Goodell believes the time has come for them to work out a deal.</p><p>“They need to find a solution for a stadium,” Goodell said Tuesday at his NFL Annual Meeting press conference. "They have looked not only in Indiana but also in Illinois at other sites, and invested in a site. I think they have been responsible in that fact.</p><p>“I think it’s really important that they come to a resolution on this relatively soon. … This is an important time to get this resolved.”</p><p>Bears chairman George McCaskey and president/CEO Kevin Warren figure to stress a similar urgency when they meet with the media Wednesday at the Arizona Biltmore. The Bears are seeking property tax cuts on the 326 acres they own in Arlington Heights and want the Illinois General Assembly to allow them to negotiate a payment-in-lieu-of-taxes. The assembly is in session through May 31, leaving just two months to work out an agreement.</p><p>Indiana looms. In late February, the state of Indiana <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/02/26/bears-stadium-hammond-indiana-hammong-senate-passes-bill-27-arlington-heights-george-mccaaskey-kevin-warren-nfl" target="_blank" >passed a bill</a> that established the Northwest Indiana Stadium Authority, which provided the vehicle to finance a Bears stadium in Hammond.</p><p>Goodell has toured stadium sites in both states and said he’s spoken recently with officials from Illinois.</p><p>Asked directly by the Sun-Times if he believed the Bears should play their home games in a different state, Goodell said the team was “looking at the best resolution,” wherever it may be.</p><p>“That’s an important element, as you know, to the success of a founding team,” he said.</p><p>Soldier Field has great tradition, he said, but lacks advancements in technology that contributes to the overall fan experience.</p><p>Goodell addressed other issues Tuesday afternoon, saying the NFL’s Rooney Rule, which aims to encourage diversity of hiring candidates, isn’t going anywhere despite threats of legal action in Florida.</p><p>Goodell said negotiations haven’t progressed as far as he’d hoped with the NFL Referees Association. The two sides are staring down a work stoppage at the end of May. The NFL passed a rule Tuesday that allows the league to use replay to fix obvious officiating mistakes if a game is being called by replacement referees this season.</p><p>Other rule changes passed Tuesday allow teams to declare an onside kick at any point during the game. The kicking team no longer has an incentive to kick the ball out of bounds when they kick off from the 50 after a penalty. The kick return team can now align slightly differently.</p><p>Goodell said there was no talk of changing the free agency legal tampering period in the wake of Maxx Crosby’s failed physical. He also said he expects NFL players to participate in flag football at the 2028 Olympics.</p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" entries.author: Patrick Finley

entries.title: South Shore Line extends service to Munster, Dyer with new stations
entries.summary: <p>Twenty-five years ago when Curtis Vosti worked for the mayor’s office in Hammond, Indiana, he and others thought of extending the South Shore Train Line into Munster, about 8 miles south from downtown Hammond.</p><p>Two decades later and nearly $2 billion in construction costs, Vosti boarded the train with his wife, Marisol, Tuesday at the new station just blocks from his home in Munster.</p><p>“I worked on this project when it was just in the imagination stage,” Vosti said. “Working in government, you know that good projects take some time to get done. This is a very proud day for everyone.”</p><p>The $1.6 billion extension is known as the Monon Corridor, named after a rail line from decades ago that is no longer in service. The Northern Indiana Commuter Transportation District (NICTD) operates the South Shore Train Line that runs east-to-west from Millennium Station in Chicago to the South Bend Airport. The new line is its first that runs north to south, reaching Munster and Dyer. </p><p>The day got off to a rough start, with threatening skies pushing back the beginning of the dedication.</p><p>“Today is a great day for the city of Hammond and for all of Northwest Indiana,” said Hammond Mayor Tom McDermott Jr., at the new station, just feet from the Illinois-Indiana state line. “We're here to celebrate the opening of the Monon corridor, a transformational expansion of the South Shore line and a major investment in our region's future.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/049c697/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Faf%2Ffa%2Fe2ebbc064abf88e4175d3f8baf91%2Fsouthshoreline-260401-032.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The South Shore Line now extends to Munster and Dyer, marking a new path for those who commute to Chicago. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Over the past decade, NICTD leveraged federal and state dollars to make significant improvements to its line — from a double-tracking project that reduces travel times between Michigan City and Gary to the new Monon Corridor. The train line’s <a class="Link" href="https://www.railwayage.com/passenger/commuterregional/south-shore-line-nictds-the-little-railroad-that-could-prospers-and-grows/" target="_blank" >history</a> dates back to the early 1900s.</p><p>“The Monon Corridor expands rail access, strengthens connectivity and positions our region for long-term growth and the impact is already being felt. This project, along with the double track expansion, is driving billions of dollars in investment, creating thousands of jobs and generating real economic momentum across our region, and here in Hammond, we're seeing it firsthand,” said Michael Noland, retiring president of the South Shore Train Line.</p><p>Besides four new train stations, the new line includes elevated train tracks to bypass freight lines in downtown Hammond. The investment in the line, Noland said, is spurring new housing and retail development.</p><p>“You're also seeing something else: attention from developers, from employers, and yes, even from organizations like the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2026/03/26/with-bears-in-mind-sanders-bill-would-offer-up-sports-teams-to-city-before-billionaire-owners-try-to-move" target="_blank" >Chicago Bears</a>, taking a serious look at Northwest Indiana,” Noland said. “Let's be clear, that kind of attention didn't happen before this project was created. This project started that momentum and we've been building this moment for years and Hammond's ready.”</p><p>Noland said the line will help grow Munster and Dyer, which are among the fastest growing communities in Northwest Indiana.</p><p>“This is a nearly $1.6 billion investment. This is not just about expanding commuter rail service in this section of state. This sends a signal to the private sector investors about Indiana's willingness to invest in large, transformational infrastructure projects that offer the opportunity to strengthen our local economy and the state's economy and greatly benefit our Hoosier quality of place and quality of life,” Noland said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-d20000" name="image-d20000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f4c35f4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3c%2Fda%2F9ce92c2444fe9db620c7ae2e6b42%2Fsouthshoreline-260401-003.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/277b630/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3c%2Fda%2F9ce92c2444fe9db620c7ae2e6b42%2Fsouthshoreline-260401-003.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/637bb77/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3c%2Fda%2F9ce92c2444fe9db620c7ae2e6b42%2Fsouthshoreline-260401-003.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Indiana Gov. Mike Braun holds a ceremonial golden ticket before boarding the first South Shore Line train ride along the Monon Corridor in Hammond. | Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/637bb77/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3c%2Fda%2F9ce92c2444fe9db620c7ae2e6b42%2Fsouthshoreline-260401-003.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/baaf0fc/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3c%2Fda%2F9ce92c2444fe9db620c7ae2e6b42%2Fsouthshoreline-260401-003.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/637bb77/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3c%2Fda%2F9ce92c2444fe9db620c7ae2e6b42%2Fsouthshoreline-260401-003.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Indiana Gov. Mike Braun holds a ceremonial golden ticket before boarding the first South Shore Line train ride along the Monon Corridor in Hammond. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Indiana Gov. Mike Braun estimates the Monon Corridor will generate some $5 billion over 20 years in new investment including 6,000 jobs. Braun wants that to include the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/02/26/bears-stadium-hammond-indiana-hammong-senate-passes-bill-27-arlington-heights-george-mccaaskey-kevin-warren-nfl" target="_blank" >Chicago Bears crossing the state line</a> and calling Hammond home.</p><p>Despite being Republican, Braun has been working closely with McDermott, a Democrat.</p><p>“Sometimes the moon, the sun, the stars line up,” Braun said. “Every time I come up, there’s a vitality here [Northwest Indiana] that we have nowhere else in the state,” Braun said. “When you finally bring this to fruition, when you look at what might happen, and even if that doesn't, there's a point where synergy takes over and we all benefit.”</p><p>Braun used a golden ticket to board the new train line as its first passenger. He rode in the lead train with Noland and other dignitaries.</p><p>Hammond resident Charles Semko was also on the train.</p><p>“I'd rather see the money spent on infrastructure than wasting it on foreign wars,” the 72-year-old said. “I think it's going to bring pride to the area, put Munster on the map. It’s going to be a great commute. I know a lot of people that work downtown, and they're going to start taking it.”</p>
entries.author: Michael Puente

entries.title: How the Cubs lured right-hander Hunter Harvey to Chicago
entries.summary: <p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs" target="_blank" >Cubs</a> reliever <b>Hunter Harvey</b> didn’t have Chicago on his radar as a free agent during the offseason.</p><p>‘‘I’ve never been a big-city person, so I’ve always liked being with the teams with cities that are as far as I can get outside of it,’’ he told the Sun-Times.</p><p>But the Cubs worked to change Harvey’s mind.</p><p>Harvey, a first-round draft pick in 2013, has dealt with a litany of injuries. He appeared in only 12 major-league games last season with the Royals because of a strained muscle in his right (pitching) shoulder and a strained adductor. When healthy, however, Harvey has been effective, as his 3.11 career ERA entering this season shows.</p><p>The Cubs have been effective in recent seasons at getting the most out of short-term relievers and setting them up for future paydays (see <b>Brad Keller</b> in 2025). But despite knowing the Cubs’ reputation, Harvey still didn’t have them high on his list.</p><p>‘‘But then I had a phone call with [pitching coach <b>Tommy Hottovy</b>] and [vice president of pitching <b>Tyler Zombro</b>] in December, and after the meeting I talked to my agent and was like, ‘The Cubs just jumped way up on my list,’ ’’ said Havery, who signed a one-year, $6 million deal. ‘‘I was very shocked.’’</p><p>The pairing will benefit both sides. The Cubs’ bullpen needed a reliever such as Harvey, who has a powerful fastball, after they lost Keller to the Phillies.</p><p>Closer <b>Daniel Palencia</b> helps in that department, as does right-hander <b>Ben Brown</b>, but Harvey’s experience and history of success attracted the Cubs, whose bullpen finished 21st in strikeouts per nine innings last season.</p><p>Harvey said that over calls and Zoom meetings, the Cubs showed him how they track players and could keep him healthy.</p><p>‘‘I was mesmerized by it,’’ he said. ‘‘They were able to keep players healthy, and it really grabbed my attention because that’s kind of one thing I’ve been battling.’’</p><p>Harvey also has dealt with a strained elbow, a strained lat and a strained flexor pronator. It would be easy for him to grow bitter about injuries, but he is pleased with the way his journey has turned out.</p><p>‘‘I’ve learned a lot about my body,’’ Harvey said. ‘‘I learned about how to get through injuries. You know, I guess it’s a step, a steppingstone in my career path.’’</p><h3>Happ’s power</h3><p>Left fielder <b>Ian Happ</b> traditionally has been a bit of a slow starter. In March/April in his career, he has had a .725 OPS. That increases in May (.793) and June (.800) and dips in July (.729) before rising again in August (.858) and September (.810).</p><p>That hasn’t been the case so far this season, though. In the Cubs’ first four games, Happ had a 1.080 OPS with three home runs.</p><p>Manager <b>Craig Counsell</b> said it’s important that Happ has gotten off to a good start because the Cubs are lacking a right-handed slugger with right fielder <b>Seiya Suzuki</b> sidelined with a sprained ligament in his right knee.</p><h3>Suzuki update</h3><p>The Cubs said Suzuki will go on a rehab assignment when they begin their six-game road trip Friday. They haven’t decided whether he’ll go to Triple-A Iowa or Double-A Tennessee or how long the assignment will be.</p><p>Before the game Tuesday, the Cubs tested Suzuki’s ability to stop and start. That was one of his final required tests.</p><bsp-listcarousel class="PageListE" data-module data-column-count=3 data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" entries.author: Kyle Williams

entries.title: Miami homecoming gives Miguel Vargas strong foundation to step up in White Sox clubhouse
entries.summary: <p>MIAMI — The sheer volume of raucous cheers inside cavernous loanDepot Park this week have belied modest Miami crowd sizes and even the team receiving those ovations.</p><p>There were only 6,515 fans there to watch Miguel Vargas walk to the plate with the bases loaded in the fourth inning Monday night. But from the roar that followed the ball he cracked into the Florida night, you might’ve thought Vargas had clubbed one 402 feet into the left field bleachers at Rate Field — or to a home crowd in his native Havana.</p><p>Then again, they’re all home games down here for Vargas, the Cuban-born Miami resident who’s just as beloved by his <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="_blank" ><u>White Sox</u></a> teammates and a front office that’s looking for him to take a significant step in his development as a clubhouse leader and a key cog of their organizational rebuild.</p><p>“You're not supposed to have favorites, but as a human, as a person, he is one of my favorites,” gushed manager Will Venable, who equally adores Vargas’ versatility in the field and in the batting order. “He's just such a personable guy. He's so caring.”</p><p>Arriving on the South Side in the Michael Kopech trade just in time for the worst days of the 2024 season’s record-breaking futility, Vargas’ numbers over his first full season last year were solid, if not necessarily screaming “franchise building block.” He hit .234/.316/.401 with 16 homers and 32 doubles in 138 games.</p><p>And the 26-year-old is off to a quality start at the plate in Year 2, if the grand slam <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/30/white-sox-vargas-grand-slam-first-win-season" target="_blank" ><u>didn’t give it away</u></a>. Through four games, Vargas was 4-for-13 with and two walks. He’s also been steady on defense, entering the year as the closest thing to an everyday third baseman for the perpetual lineup shuffler Venable.</p><p>Vargas got his first homer of the year while playing first base and he’s got no small ambitions wherever he lines up. Just ask him about his <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/22/white-sox-miguel-vargas-working-hard-to-become-complete-third-baseman" target="_blank" ><u>desire to win a Gold Glove</u></a>.</p><p>“Definitely trying to be the best I can,” said Vargas, who played as a teenager for the Industriales de La Habana in the Cuban National League.</p><div class="Enhancement" 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/955c402/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4093x2297+0+0/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F14%2Fcb%2F3341bfbe4eab9438b96b7840243c%2Fwhite-sox-marlins-baseball.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/515820c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4093x2297+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F14%2Fcb%2F3341bfbe4eab9438b96b7840243c%2Fwhite-sox-marlins-baseball.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Miguel Vargas (left) and Austin Hays celebrate after defeating the Miami Marlins Monday.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Marta Lavandier/AP </p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>His father, Lázaro Vargas, won two Olympic gold medals with Cuba in the 1990s, adding to the interest in Miguel’s career back home. English-speaking reporters are outnumbered in the clubhouse this week by Spanish-speaking media catching up with Vargas and other Sox with local ties like catcher Edgar Quero and rookie reliever Jedixson Paez, along with the sizable Japanese press corps following Vargas’ new friend and teammate Munetaka Murakami.</p><p>Vargas took his fellow first/third baseman under his wing at spring training, and borrowed Murakami’s first base mitt on Monday.</p><p>“We've been doing a really good job, especially trusting the guy next to you, behind you, getting on base and doing the right things for winning baseball games,” Vargas said. “I think we're in the right spot right now.”</p><p>After his career-high six-RBI night — which automatically put him in an early tie as the MLB leader entering play Tuesday — Vargas caught up with dozens of relatives and friends in the stands. “They've been supporting me my entire career, and I love playing in front of them,” he said.</p><p>This stage of his career finds Vargas evolving into a team leader, Venable said. “Once you establish yourself in this league, you get a little more time under your belt, you perform a little better, your vocal leadership has the ability to grow a little more.”</p><p>Vargas’ other new teammate, Austin Hays, said he recognized that when he walked in the clubhouse.</p><p>“He’s one of those guys, he’s like a light anywhere he goes,” the veteran outfielder said. “You’ve got to have guys like that in the clubhouse… He’s definitely one of those guys that is always going to keep everybody up and keep everybody going.”</p><p>But it’s not just the charisma. “He’s got a lot of power to all sides of the field, he’s got pretty good plate discipline,” Hays said. “I think he can put up some really good numbers.”</p><p></p><div class="PageListL" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-show-loadmore="true" ><a class="AnchorLink" id="list-4a0005" entries.title: Cook County warns of potential measles exposures at O'Hare, Niles and Mount Prospect
entries.summary: <p>The Cook County Department of Public Health is warning of a possible measles exposures in Chicago's northwest suburbs.</p><p>The possible exposure took place last week. The virus can remain in the air for up to two hours after the infectious person has left the area, though transmission requires just 15 minutes of exposure, the health agency said.</p><p>The possible exposure locations are:<br></p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" id="rte-47dca0e2-2d49-11f1-81da-01c5afa0ee41"><li>O'Hare International Airport's Terminal 5, March 26 from 10:45 a.m. to 2:30 p.m.</li><li>Fresh Farms, 8203 W. Golf Rd., Niles March 26 from 7:30 p.m. to 9:00 p.m.</li><li>Marshalls, 8249 W. Golf Rd., Niles March 26 from 8 p.m. to 9:30 p.m. </li></ul><p>The person also visited Endeavor Health Immediate Care Center in Mount Prospect last Friday, and county officials are working to reach people who may have been in exposed. </p><p>Those most at risk are babies, young children, pregnant individuals and people with compromised immune systems. Vaccinated individuals are typically not at risk since two doses provides 97% protection. The health agency advises those who may have been exposed consult with a health care provider. </p><p>People experiencing symptoms — rash, high fever, cough, runny nose, and red, watery eyes — should call a health care provider before going to a clinic to help control the spread of the virus, the agency said. Measles symptoms appear up to 21 days following exposure, as well as four days before and four days after the onset of the rash, which starts on the face and spreads to the body.</p><p>Illinois saw 14 measles cases in 2025 <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2025/07/11/illinois-measles-outbreak-over-state-health-department-officials" target="_blank" ><u>during its peak</u></a> — when nearly 1,300 cases cropped up nationwide, the worst outbreak in more than 30 years. It was a sharp drop from the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2024/05/30/chicago-measles-outbreak-declared-over-health-department" target="_blank" ><u>three-month measles outbreak in Chicago</u></a>, where 64 cases were confirmed in 2024.</p><p>Those not vaccinated should talk to their provider about getting it, as doing so may prevent measles if administered within 72 hours after exposure, the health agency said. An appointment with a Cook County Health clinic can be made by calling (312) 864-1111.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2026/02/17/illinois-reports-first-measles-case-of-2026" >Illinois reports first measles case of 2026</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Bears coach Ben Johnson's objective take on his defense: Lots of work to do
entries.summary: <p>PHOENIX — The uncomfortable truth for <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >the Bears</a>’ defense is that it wasn’t as good as it might’ve looked last season. They made many big plays, but they didn’t make the consistent, necessary ones reflective of the best units in the NFL.</p><p>While coach Ben Johnson didn’t say that harshly when discussing his defense at the NFL annual meeting, reading between the lines of his comments, it was clear he knew it.</p><p>He mentioned that if defensive coordinator Dennis Allen had been sitting next to him, Allen would say the Bears have “a number of categories where we have to improve” despite leading the league with 33 takeaways. Coaches often like to highlight their emphasis on forcing turnovers and downplay randomness, but Johnson conceded takeaways are “not a sustainable metric.”</p><p>He added, “Our run defense wasn’t good enough,” and, “We gave up too many explosive plays,” both of which were true. He didn’t expound much on the struggling pass rush, but noted the media attention on that aspect of the roster and said, “I got it.”</p><p>Righting the defense is imperative in the quest to leap from good to great and essential to quarterback Caleb Williams’ development. The blueprint cannot be to win games 47-42 and leave Williams frequently playing from behind because his defense can’t stop anyone.</p><p>Fixing it starts with acknowledging it, so that’s a key step by Johnson as he continues to be relentlessly objective about the Bears in a way that predecessors Matt Eberflus and Matt Nagy weren’t.</p><p>It’s also a good sign that he has more of a global grasp of the organization than either of those two. In his first year-plus on the job, Johnson has spoken authoritatively on everything from salary-cap management to the defensive scheme despite having minimal experience in either area.</p><p>The Bears gave up the fourth-most total yards in the NFL and were in the bottom third in pressure rate, but bailed some of that water out of the boat with 23 interceptions and 10 fumble recoveries, as well as a decent red-zone defense that allowed touchdowns on 56.1% of trips by the opponent, which ranked 15th.</p><p>That’s not typically a winning equation. Three of the last four teams standing in the recent playoffs — the Broncos, Patriots and champion Seahawks — were top-eight in total defense, and the Rams were in the middle at 17th. All were top-12 in run defense. The Broncos, Rams and Seahawks were top-six in pressure rate.</p><p>Those teams were all over the map in takeaways, ranging from the Rams at No. 5 to the Broncos at No. 26, but they weren’t dependent on them. They could win the boring way, simply by shutting down the run and making the opposing quarterback miserable.</p><p>When the Bears got one or no takeaways last season, they allowed 29.3 points per game and went 2-6. Those final four teams still went 30-11 in those scenarios.</p><p>With a salary-cap squeeze this offseason, there’s little recourse other than improvement from within. The Bears added a dynamic, young safety in Coby Bryant and hope for minimal dropoff from former linebacker Tremaine Edmunds to replacement Devin Bush, but otherwise, Johnson is banking on his coaching staff getting more out of the existing core.</p><p>That’s especially true up front, where general manager Ryan Poles has used significant resources but gotten modest results.</p><p>He added defensive tackle Grady Jarrett and defensive end Dayo Odeyingbo on contracts totaling $91.3 million, but neither played up to the projection. Defensive tackle Gervon Dexter, a 2023 second-round pick, has been fine, but not a game-changer. Similarly, defensive end <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2025/09/04/bears-de-montez-sweat-out-to-reassert-himself-as-elite-pass-rusher-but-more-so-he-wants-to-finally-win" >Montez Sweat</a> has been good, but as the team’s highest-paid player, he needs to be great.</p><p>Johnson is an offensive-minded coach and doesn’t micromanage Allen, but the defense is his responsibility regardless. He must be as good of a problem solver on that side of the ball as he is on his own.</p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-show-loadmore="true" ><a class="AnchorLink" id="list-bc0001" name="list-bc0001"></a><div class="PageList-header" > <div class="PageList-header-title-wrap" > <div class="PageList-header-title"> <a class="" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="{borderColor=#dfdfdf, pageNumber=1, items=[{publishDate=March 31, 2026 05:08 PM, type=Image, _styledTemplate=null, url=https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/31/bears-coach-ben-johnsons-objective-take-on-his-defense-lots-of-work-to-do, authors=[{jobTitle=[{items=[Chicago Bears reporter]}], ampLink=null, _styledTemplate=null, _template=/author/byline/AuthorByline.hbs, authorPageUrl=https://chicago.suntimes.com/authors/jason-lieser, name=[{items=[Jason Lieser]}], bio=[{items=[Jason Lieser covers the Chicago Bears for the Chicago Sun-Times.]}], media=[{image={src=https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/db/45/4fa7d0e60b81cf16ace05e7c41e9/f5kj4hj-400x400-0.jpg, width=512, height=512}, narrowImage={src=https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/db/45/4fa7d0e60b81cf16ace05e7c41e9/f5kj4hj-400x400-0.jpg, width=512, height=512}, ampLink=null, _styledTemplate=null, _template=/image/Image.hbs, alt=Headshot of Chicago Bears reporter Jason Lieser}]}], imageAlignment=null, isFeatured=false, target=null, primaryMediaType=null, timestamp=1774994892000, categoryUrl=https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears, description=[{items=[The Bears came through with big plays, but were unsteady throughout last season and struggled with basics like stopping the run and pressuring the quarterback. entries.title: Why did the Blackhawks practice fighting? To 'learn how to protect ourselves'
entries.summary: <p>If you expect a punch to come from your left and it instead comes from your right, the outcome can be disastrous.</p><p>How to avoid that scenario is one of many things some <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks" target="_blank" >Blackhawks</a> learned during an unofficial ‘‘fighting practice’’ Monday with former NHL enforcer Wade Brookbank, now a Hawks pro scout.</p><p>Defensemen Alex Vlasic, Louis Crevier, Ethan Del Mastro and Sam Rinzel and forwards Landon Slaggert and Sacha Boisvert attended the on-ice session.</p><p>‘‘It’s a lot about just protecting yourself,’’ Slaggert said Tuesday. ‘‘You’re not there to chuck bombs, necessarily, but [we practiced how] to limit guys’ range of motion with different grabs and holds on the jersey. And some different techniques to get out of bad situations, like if you’re fighting a lefty. It was valuable.’’</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">The Blackhawks held a fight camp this morning with Vlasic, Crevier, Rinzel, Del Mastro, Boisvert, and Slaggert in attendance: <a href="https://t.co/643OX4txwe">pic.twitter.com/643OX4txwe</a></p>— BHF (@BlackhawksFocus) <a href="https://twitter.com/BlackhawksFocus/status/2038659829791613284?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 30, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>The Hawks’ three most experienced fighters — Connor Murphy, Nick Foligno and Colton Dach — were shipped out at the trade deadline, leaving the team without anything close to an enforcer.</p><p>The prevalence of fighting in the NHL is decreasing, and the prevailing logic about enforcers has changed. Analytics-focused organizations no longer consider it worth dedicating a roster spot solely to someone such as Brookbank, who racked up 345 penalty minutes in 127 NHL games from 2003 to 2009.</p><p></p><p>But it’s still important for players to have the skills to stand up for one another, when necessary, as well as to protect themselves if they get jumped by an opponent.</p><p></p><p>‘‘Something you take away from the really good teams in this league is, they’re a little scrappy and tenacious and not afraid to mix it up sometimes,’’ Vlasic said.</p><p></p><p>‘‘For us, it was important — as the big guys — to learn how to protect ourselves in a fight when it comes up or whenever we need to. It was a really fun opportunity to learn some things and push Louis around a little bit. I had a lot of fun, honestly.’’</p><p></p><p>As you can imagine, Vlasic winked in Crevier’s direction during the middle of those comments.</p><p></p><p>Vlasic took some heat for not fighting Rangers captain J.T. Miller after Miller leveled defenseman Artyom Levshunov last week <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/03/27/blackhawks-young-core-gut-check-rangers-flyers-kyle-davidson" target="_blank" >at Madison Square Garden</a>. Vlasic later explained he noticed a delayed penalty had been called, and he made the quick mental calculation that the power play was more valuable than retribution.</p><p></p><p>Fighting has been a hot topic lately around the league, too, in the wake of Ducks defenseman Radko Gudas’ knee-on-knee hit that tore Maple Leafs star Auston Matthews’ medial collateral ligament March 12. No Leafs fought Gudas at the time, but Max Domi did off the opening faceoff of the teams’ rematch Monday.</p><p></p><p>Coach Jeff Blashill insisted the timing of the Hawks’ fighting practice had nothing to do with either storyline, though. The team had hoped to arrange it earlier in the season, but it proved difficult to find a window in Brookbank’s schedule.</p><p></p><p>‘‘We have [written] above our locker room, ‘Fight for your teammates,’ ’’ Blashill said. ‘‘That’s literally and figuratively. It doesn’t necessarily mean you have to fight. It just means you have to care enough about each other that you’re willing to do that.’’</p><p></p><p>Vlasic’s moment of pacifism was a rarity for the Hawks this season. Ever since Connor Bedard <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2025/10/29/connor-bedard-blackhawks-voice-honest-kings-boring-macklin-celebrini" target="_blank" >mentioned</a> after the second game of the season that they were ‘‘not going to take any [expletive]’’ anymore, almost every big hit on a Hawk has incited a scrum.</p><p></p><p>But that hot-headed approach has canceled out plenty of power-play opportunities. The Hawks entered play Tuesday with 278 penalties called against them, compared with 268 against opponents.</p><p></p><p>‘‘In some of those moments, we probably didn’t [respond] the smart way in [the] sense that, the minute [you] go right in, you’re getting the extra penalty,’’ Blashill said. ‘‘Really savvy players who have been around the league know to wait a shift and then maybe challenge [the offender].’’</p><p></p><p>Indeed, there’s a strategy to fighting.</p>
entries.author: Ben Pope
